performance_anti_patterns_avoided: No quantization or pitch correction (1994 era + Beinhorn philosophy preserves performance integrity)
Vocal NOT comped piecewise — full takes preferred
No second-take overdubs added beyond live tracking + chorus vocal layering

recording.studio: Bad Animals Studio, Seattle, Washington (Heart's studio — documented Superunknown tracking location)
recording.primary_engineer_producer: Michael Beinhorn (producer) — different from Badmotorfinger's Terry Date. Beinhorn's documented portfolio includes Soundgarden Superunknown, Korn, Marilyn Manson, Ozzy Osbourne. Brings a more PRODUCER-DRIVEN approach than Date's tracking-focused style.
recording.subsequent_remix: Brendan O'Brien mixed Superunknown — separate engineer from producer (in contrast to Date who tracked AND mixed his Soundgarden production)

drum_micing_approach.philosophy_beinhorn: Multiple close mics + room mics + overheads — Bad Animals's tracking room is medium-sized, captures kit with controlled ambience. Beinhorn's approach more compositionally-aware than Date's pure-capture preference.
specifics_documented: Bad Animals tracking room used — Heart's studio with documented strong drum acoustics
Cameron's restrained playing on FOBD captured cleanly with close + room balance
Compression at tracking applied but more subtly than Date or Wallace would (Beinhorn's preference)
Hi-hat and cymbal positioning carefully captured for the verse arpeggio interplay

guitar_tracking_approach.philosophy_beinhorn: Beinhorn encouraged Soundgarden's TWO-GUITAR layering approach — Thayil and Cornell tracked separately for full stereo guitar field
specifics_documented: Both guitars in drop D
Multiple takes tracked per guitar for chorus distortion thickness
Tube amps (Soundgarden's documented amp options for Superunknown era — Mesa Boogie + Marshall variations)
Verse: clean amp tones tracked for arpeggio texture (separate amp settings from chorus distortion takes)

bass_tracking_approach.specifics: DI + amp blend per Bad Animals standard. Shepherd's melodic bass role required clarity in mix — documented as recorded with care for melodic intelligibility (vs JCP's deeper-in-mix bass)

vocal_tracking_approach.philosophy_beinhorn: Vocal isolation booth at Bad Animals — clean capture for Cornell's full range. Multiple complete passes recorded per take.
specifics_documented: Cornell tracked in vocal booth, dynamic mic capable of high SPL (similar to Shure SM7 / AKG D series)
Beinhorn documented as preferring multiple complete vocal takes then selection rather than comping piecewise
Doubled chorus vocals: Cornell self-doubled across multiple takes (Soundgarden's documented chorus harmony approach)

notable_instruments_and_gear_documented: Bad Animals Studio's Neve console (the studio's documented main console)
Cornell's vocal mic — likely Shure SM7 (handles high SPL and dynamic range)
what_makes_beinhorn_recording_distinct: More PRODUCER-DRIVEN than Date — Beinhorn shaped songs more actively in tracking decisions
Captures BOTH band live performance AND allows for compositional refinement via multi-take selection
Bad Animals room character distinct from Studio D Sausalito — Pacific Northwest studio aesthetic vs California metal-friendly studio
O'Brien's separate mixing role allowed Beinhorn to focus on tracking; gave Superunknown a more polished mix character than the Date-self-mix Badmotorfinger
recording.beinhorn_vs_date_for_soundgarden: Date (Badmotorfinger 1991) tracked AND mixed — capture-focused, raw. Beinhorn (Superunknown 1994) tracked with more producer involvement, then handed off to O'Brien for mixing — more polished, more compositionally shaped. The two albums sound different partly because of these production philosophy differences applied to the same band.

mixing.history_documented: SINGLE released mix version: Brendan O'Brien mixed Superunknown. O'Brien is DIFFERENT from the producer (Beinhorn) — separation of tracking and mixing roles, in contrast to Date's tracker-mixer dual role on Badmotorfinger.
obrien_mix_characteristics_for_fobd: MODERATE compression — neither Albini's pure capture nor Wallace's heavy commercial — O'Brien's signature middle ground
Verse texture preserved: clean guitar arpeggios sit in a NATURAL space (light reverb, room ambience kept)
Chorus DISTORTION TIGHTENED via mix compression — clean-to-distorted dynamic shift made more dramatic via mix processing
Vocal CENTERED, present but not commercially-forward — Cornell's vocal sits IN the band rather than ON TOP of it
Two-guitar stereo separation: Thayil panned slightly off-center, Cornell rhythm guitar mirrored on opposite side (less hard-panned than Date's JCP approach)
Drum balance: close mics dominant, room mics blended subtly for texture (between Albini's room emphasis and Wallace's close-mic focus)
mixing.released_version_mix_engineer: Brendan O'Brien at his mixing room
mixing.console_documented: O'Brien's documented preference for SSL G-series consoles in his mid-90s mixes
mixing.compression_philosophy_obrien_for_fobd: Selective compression — vocal compressed for consistency, drum bus moderately compressed (not heavy like Date's JCP), guitar bus left more dynamic. The compression supports the song's CLEAN-TO-DISTORTED shift by NOT crushing the verse's quiet dynamics.
stereo_image_strategy_for_fobd: Drums: kick + snare CENTERED, hi-hat slightly off-center, room mics blended NARROWER than Date's JCP (less aggressive room spread)
Guitar 2 (Cornell rhythm): panned MODERATELY right (matching Thayil's depth)
Vocal CENTERED with subtle width via doubled chorus vocals slightly spread
mixing.vocal_treatment: Cornell vocal: moderate compression (preserves dynamic delivery), subtle plate or chamber reverb adding 'space' that suits the introspective song character, mild EQ presence boost for vocal clarity in mid-register
mixing.drum_treatment: Close-mic dominant balance, light parallel compression on drum bus (less aggressive than Date or Wallace), room mics blended for ambient context but not featured (vs Albini's room emphasis)
mixing.what_obrien_mix_demonstrates: O'Brien's mix supports the CLEAN-TO-DISTORTED dynamic SHIFT compositional intent. Heavy compression (Wallace) would FLATTEN the dynamic shift between verse and chorus by crushing the verse's quiet dynamics up to chorus level. Pure capture (Albini) would leave the chorus distortion uncontrolled. O'Brien's moderate compression is OPTIMAL for this kind of dynamic-shift composition.
mixing.comparison_to_jcp_mix: JCP (Date heavy compression) for sustained intensity = compression CONTAINS chaos. FOBD (O'Brien moderate compression) for dynamic shift = compression PRESERVES dynamic shift between sections. Same band, two different mix philosophies serving two different compositional intents.

mastering.mastering_engineer_album: Stephen Marcussen at Marcussen Mastering — documented Superunknown mastering engineer
mastering.loudness_approach_documented: 1994 mid-loudness-war era. Marcussen's master pushed Superunknown to commercial-loud levels but preserved more dynamic range than late-90s peak loudness. The album sits at moderate-to-high loudness for 1994 alt-rock.
mastering.vinyl_vs_cd_documented: Original CD master by Marcussen. Vinyl release used different mastering pass.
mastering.subsequent_remasters_documented: Various reissues over the decades. 25th anniversary edition (2014) remastered.
mastering.tonal_balance_at_release: Bright top end (matches O'Brien's mix character), present mid-range for Cornell's vocal, controlled low-end. Tonal balance distinct from Badmotorfinger's Weinberg master — Superunknown sits slightly more polished and slightly less aggressive in mastering.
mastering.marcussen_vs_weinberg_for_soundgarden: Weinberg mastered Badmotorfinger (1991, raw aggressive aesthetic). Marcussen mastered Superunknown (1994, more polished aesthetic). The two albums' different mastering engineers contribute to their distinct sounds — same band, different tonal aesthetics across releases.

the_moment_people_remember.primary: The METER SHIFT from 6/4 verse to 4/4 chorus — listener feels the meter resolution as part of the chorus emotional payoff. The shift is so well-integrated that many listeners don't consciously perceive the meter change but feel its emotional effect.
the_moment_people_remember.secondary: Cornell's CHORUS VOCAL — the lift from intimate verse to opened-up chorus, matching the lyrical theme of breaking through depression. One of his most emotionally accessible vocal performances.
the_moment_people_remember.tertiary: The SUS CHORD VOICINGS in verses — the open-quality chord coloring (drop D pedal tone under sus2/sus4 voicings) creates the song's introspective harmonic identity.

advisor_extract.description: Verses in 6/4 (asymmetric, patient feel — each bar has 6 beats, longer than 4/4), choruses in 4/4 (resolves the asymmetry — chorus FEELS settled). Listener experiences chorus as RELEASE because the meter itself resolves. Distinct from the inverse direction (Outshined: 4/4 verse → 7/4 chorus = chorus DESTABILIZES). Direction depends on whether you want chorus as RELEASE or as TENSION.
use_for: Choruses that should feel like emotional resolution
compositions where meter is part of the dynamic device
songs wanting subtle metric sophistication without overt prog complexity
advisor_extract.implementation_for_modern_compositions: In MIDI/sample context: define verse in 6/4 (set time signature explicitly to 6/4 for the verse clip), chorus in 4/4. Most DAWs handle multi-meter clips natively. The transition between sections must land on the chord change for the meter resolution to register emotionally.

advisor_extract.description: In drop-tuned guitar (drop D, drop C#), use the low open string as a sustained PEDAL TONE while moving sus2/sus4 voicings on upper strings. The pedal stays constant; the voicings move around it. Creates harmonic richness without committing to chord quality (sus = neither maj nor min).
use_for: Verse arrangements wanting harmonic sophistication without density
introspective songs needing chord ambiguity
compositions where the chord progression should feel UNRESOLVED until chorus
advisor_extract.implementation_for_modern_compositions: In MIDI: set lowest voice (bass or guitar low string) on root pedal tone (e.g., D in drop D). Upper voices play sus2 (root + 2 + 5) or sus4 (root + 4 + 5) shapes that move while the pedal sustains. Avoid 3rd in voicings during pedal sections — let the chorus introduce 3rd for tonal commitment.

advisor_extract.description: Verse uses CLEAN guitar tone (arpeggios or strummed clean voicings). Chorus engages distortion via amp gain or pedal stomp. Direct dynamic shift, distinct from sustained-distortion (Very Ape) or HSB's slow-burn arrival. The shift is CLEAR section marker — listener immediately knows verse vs chorus territory.
use_for: Traditional verse-chorus compositions
songs wanting clear emotional sections without ambiguity
compositions where the chorus needs to feel ARRIVED-AT rather than gradually-grown-into
advisor_extract.implementation_for_modern_compositions: In sample/synth context: verse uses CLEAN amp simulation or undriven synth tone. Chorus engages saturation/distortion plugin or distorted preset. The transition must be on a section boundary downbeat for the shift to register. Pair with vocal layering increase (single verse vocal → doubled chorus vocal) for additional dynamic distinction.

advisor_extract.description: When mixing songs with dynamic shifts between sections (clean verse → distorted chorus), apply MODERATE compression: enough to control the chorus distortion's chaos but NOT enough to crush the verse's quiet dynamics. Heavy compression (Wallace) would FLATTEN the shift; pure capture (Albini) would leave the chorus chaotic.
use_for: Mix decisions on traditional dynamic-shift compositions
songs where verse and chorus must remain dynamically distinct in the mix
albums where the listener's perception of 'chorus arrival' matters
advisor_extract.philosophical_note: The advisor must select compression INTENSITY based on the song's compositional dynamic philosophy. Sustained intensity = heavy compression. Dynamic shift = moderate compression. Dynamic restraint = minimal compression. Three intensities for three compositional intents.

advisor_extract.description: When a vocalist has a wide range, use it SELECTIVELY rather than maximally. Verses in mid-register (intimate), choruses opening up to upper register (lift), but NOT pushing to physical limits unless the song's intensity demands it. Distinct from JCP's full-range exploitation — here the range is a TOOL deployed strategically rather than a SHOWCASE used continuously.
use_for: Songs centered on vocal expression rather than vocal showcase
introspective tracks where emotional accessibility matters more than physical performance impressiveness
compositions where the vocalist's range supports the song without dominating
advisor_extract.philosophical_note: Cornell could sing JCP's high-range exploitation OR FOBD's selective range deployment. Same vocalist, two compositional approaches. The advisor should select range usage based on song's emotional intent, not just vocalist's capability.

My_Wave.time_signature: 5/4 verses (commonly analyzed) with 4/4 chorus area — meter shift signature
My_Wave.key_area: E minor area (Aeolian) — drop D tuning common Superunknown standard
My_Wave._special_status: Track 2 on Superunknown (post-Let Me Drown opener). Mid-tempo aggressive but with playful 5/4 lilt — different mood from FOBD's introspective patience. Demonstrates Soundgarden's ability to make ODD METER FEEL NATURAL via groove construction.

composition.structural_blueprint: intro_4bars (riff in 5/4 establishes meter) -> verse1_8bars_in_5/4 -> chorus_8bars_in_4/4 (meter resolves) -> verse2_8bars_in_5/4 -> chorus_8bars_in_4/4 -> bridge_8bars (instrumental, returns to 5/4 or hybrid feel) -> chorus_extended_outro_in_4/4 -> abrupt or fade. Total ~5:11.

section_count_and_lengths.verses: 8 bars each in 5/4 (5 beats × 8 bars = 40 beats per verse)
section_count_and_lengths.choruses: 8 bars each in 4/4 (32 beats per chorus — feels SHORTER than verse despite same bar count)

composition.harmonic_motion: Modal Aeolian — minor key power chord vocabulary. The riff is harmonically simple (root + bVII or i + bVI cycle family) — the COMPOSITIONAL identity is the meter, not the harmony. Drop D tuning emphasized via low D root.
composition.melodic_contour: Verse vocal: phrases that fit the 5-beat bar feel — slightly elongated phrasing matching the asymmetric meter. Chorus vocal: more conventional ascending arc fitting 4/4. Outro: continues chorus contour.
composition.characteristic_compositional_choice: 5/4 VERSE THAT FEELS NATURAL. Where many odd-meter songs sound prog-y or academic, My Wave's 5/4 verse feels GROOVY — like a slightly stretched 4/4 rather than a deliberate metric oddity. This is achieved via groove construction (the riff lands such that the 'extra beat' feels like a natural pickup rather than an interruption). Sophisticated odd-meter implementation.
composition.harmonic_pacing: Moderate — chord changes within riff structure
composition.phrase_symmetry: 8-bar phrasing throughout. The asymmetry is in the METER (5/4 vs 4/4) not in phrase length.
composition.comparison_to_other_soundgarden_meter_sh: Spoonman (7/4 V → 4/4 C — odd meter feels DESTABILIZING). Outshined (4/4 V → 7/4 C — chorus feels DESTABILIZING). FOBD (6/4 V → 4/4 C — patient + introspective). My Wave (5/4 V → 4/4 C — GROOVY + playful). Same family of meter-shift devices applied with different emotional intents per song.

instrumentation_complete: electric guitar 1 (Kim Thayil)
electric guitar 2 (Chris Cornell)
bass guitar (Ben Shepherd)
drum kit (Matt Cameron)
lead vocal (Chris Cornell)
minimal documented overdubs beyond live tracking

section_instrumentation.intro: Riff alone or with subtle drum entry — establishes 5/4 meter immediately
section_instrumentation.verse: Full band locked to 5/4 riff, vocal enters
section_instrumentation.chorus: Meter shifts to 4/4, full band intensity, vocal opens up
section_instrumentation.bridge: Instrumental — guitar work over rhythm section

arrangement.vocal_layering_strategy: VERSE: SINGLE-tracked Cornell. CHORUS: doubled or harmonized (Soundgarden standard).
arrangement.dynamic_arc_overall: MODERATE-to-FULL intensity throughout. Verse and chorus both engage distortion — distinct from FOBD's clean-to-distorted shift. The METER shift is the primary section marker, not dynamic shift. Sustained intensity but with metric variation.

instrumental_role_assignment.drums_role: Cameron's drumming locks the 5/4 verse groove — kick + snare patterns adapted to 5-beat feel. Critical for making the odd meter feel natural rather than awkward.
instrumental_role_assignment.guitar_role: Riff carrier — the 5/4 riff IS the verse identity. Two-guitar layering as standard Soundgarden approach.
instrumental_role_assignment.bass_role: Shepherd locks with kick on the 5/4 groove
instrumental_role_assignment.vocal_role: Cornell's vocal adapts to the 5/4 phrasing — phrases stretched slightly to fit the asymmetric bar

harmonic_density_per_section.verse: Full band (5 instruments + vocal) at moderate-to-high density
harmonic_density_per_section.chorus: Full band at high density (slight intensity push from verse)

arrangement_anti_patterns_avoided_or_inv: Does NOT use clean-to-distorted dynamic shift like FOBD — both V and C engage distortion
Does NOT have introspective slow tempo like FOBD — mid-tempo aggressive
Does NOT use sustained intensity like JCP — uses meter shift as section marker
Does NOT make 5/4 sound prog/academic — groove construction makes it feel natural
arrangement.what_makes_unique_within_soundgarden: My Wave's 5/4 verse demonstrates Soundgarden's mastery of GROOVE-NATURAL ODD METER. While Spoonman uses 7/4 destabilizingly and FOBD uses 6/4 patiently, My Wave makes 5/4 feel like a slightly extended 4/4 — the asymmetry registers but doesn't disrupt body movement. This requires precise groove construction by the rhythm section.

performance.tempo_feel_description: ~118 BPM mid-tempo. Tracked LIVE per Beinhorn's Superunknown approach. The 5/4 verse demands TIGHT lock between Cameron and Shepherd — any timing slip would dissolve the groove. Documented as one of the more rehearsed grooves on the album.

drum_style.feel: Cameron's playing is the foundation that MAKES the 5/4 feel natural. He plays with confidence and groove rather than treating the meter as a counting exercise.
techniques_documented: Verse 5/4: kick + snare pattern adapted to 5-beat bar — likely grouped 3+2 or 2+3 depending on phrase
The 'extra beat' (vs 4/4) is incorporated into the groove naturally rather than highlighted
Chorus 4/4: returns to standard backbeat — listener feels the meter resolution as physical relief
Hi-hat and ride patterns adapted per section meter
Sparse fills — Cameron serves the groove over showcasing
drum_style.groove_feel_alignment: ON beat with confident pocket — the 5/4 demands precision
drum_style.fills_and_transitions: Minimal; section transitions handled via meter shift itself rather than drum fills

guitar_style.performer_thayil: Kim Thayil — primary riff carrier in 5/4 verse, distorted
guitar_style.performer_cornell: Chris Cornell — second guitar layered with Thayil

feel_per_section.chorus: Meter shifts to 4/4, riff variant in 4/4 feel

techniques_documented: Drop D tuning with low D pedal tone in riff
Both guitars distorted throughout (vs FOBD's clean-to-distorted shift)
Riff lands such that the 5-beat bar feels groovy rather than asymmetric
Two-guitar stereo layering standard Soundgarden approach
guitar_style.playing_style_versus_fobd: FOBD = clean-to-distorted dynamic shift between sections. My Wave = sustained distortion, meter shift as section marker. Same album, same production team, same band — different compositional approach.

bass_performance.role: Locked TIGHTLY with Cameron's kick on the 5/4 groove. The 5/4 verse requires bass-drum lock to feel natural — any drift would expose the asymmetry as awkward. Shepherd's playing here is documented as some of his most precise rhythmic work.
bass_performance.context: Where FOBD showcased Shepherd's MELODIC capability, My Wave showcases his RHYTHMIC PRECISION. Two faces of the same player on the same album.

vocal_delivery.verse_delivery: Mid-register, fitting phrases into the 5/4 bar. Cornell's phrasing slightly stretched to match meter.
vocal_delivery.chorus_delivery: Opens up to upper-mid register, more conventional 4/4 phrasing
documented_vocal_quirks: Less vocal strain than JCP — moderate range deployment matching the song's groove-oriented character
Vocal phrasing adapts to meter without sounding awkward — Cornell's compositional skill on display
Multiple complete takes selected per Beinhorn approach

performance_anti_patterns_avoided: No quantization (would destroy the 5/4's natural groove feel)
Tracked LIVE — band lock essential for 5/4 to function

recording.studio: Bad Animals Studio, Seattle, Washington (same as FOBD)
recording.subsequent_remix: Brendan O'Brien mixed Superunknown — same as FOBD, separate engineer from producer

drum_micing_approach.philosophy_beinhorn: Same as FOBD — close mics + room mics + overheads at Bad Animals's medium-sized tracking room
specifics_for_my_wave: 5/4 groove demands clean drum capture so the meter pattern reads clearly
Cameron's pattern recorded with priority on kick + snare clarity
Subtle compression at tracking — Beinhorn's preference

guitar_tracking_approach.philosophy_beinhorn: Two-guitar layering (Thayil + Cornell tracked separately, panned wide)
specifics_documented: Both guitars in drop D with distortion engaged throughout (vs FOBD's clean-verse approach)
Multiple takes per guitar for chorus thickness
Tube amps (Mesa Boogie / Marshall variations from Superunknown era)

bass_tracking_approach.specifics: DI + amp blend. Shepherd's RHYTHMIC PRECISION on the 5/4 groove demanded clean capture for tight lock with kick.

vocal_tracking_approach.philosophy_beinhorn: Vocal isolation booth, multiple complete takes
specifics_documented: Cornell tracked in vocal booth at Bad Animals
5/4 verse phrasing required multiple takes to find optimal vocal placement within asymmetric bars
Chorus self-doubling tracked as separate complete passes

notable_instruments_and_gear_documented: Cameron's full kit (critical for 5/4 groove feel)
what_makes_recording_distinct_from_fobd: Same album + production team but DIFFERENT compositional intent — recording emphasizes RHYTHMIC PRECISION (5/4 lock) over INTROSPECTIVE TEXTURE (FOBD's sus voicings + clean arpeggios)
Drums tracked with priority on kick-snare clarity for meter readability
Bass tracked with priority on rhythmic lock (vs FOBD's melodic clarity priority)
Vocal tracked with attention to phrasing fit in 5/4 (multiple takes for placement)

mixing.history_documented: SINGLE released mix version: Brendan O'Brien at his mixing room. Same engineer as FOBD but different mix decisions per song's needs.
obrien_mix_characteristics_for_my_wave: Drum bus compression TIGHTER than FOBD — kick + snare punch prioritized so the 5/4 groove reads clearly through the mix
Guitars panned wide with both takes engaged distortion — 5/4 riff dominates the stereo field
Bass slightly more forward in mix than FOBD (Shepherd's rhythmic precision deserved clarity)
Cornell vocal centered, present but not commercially-loud — sits in the band
5/4 verse: less reverb than FOBD's verses (clarity over atmosphere)
4/4 chorus: room ambience opens up slightly for the dynamic lift
mixing.compression_philosophy_obrien_for_my_wav: TIGHTER drum compression than FOBD — the 5/4 groove demands kick + snare punch be locked-tight in the mix so the meter reads clearly. Vocal compression similar to FOBD. Master glue similar.
stereo_image_strategy: Drums: kick + snare CENTERED, room mics blended NARROWER than aggressive Date approach
Bass CENTERED with kick — combined low end punch
mixing.vocal_treatment: Cornell vocal: moderate compression for consistency, subtle reverb. Less reverb than FOBD's chamber-style space — My Wave wants clarity for the meter to register.
mixing.drum_treatment: Close-mic dominant balance with parallel compression for 5/4 groove punch. Less room ambience than FOBD.
mixing.what_obrien_mix_demonstrates_for_my_wave: Same engineer adapting mix philosophy per song. FOBD: moderate compression preserving dynamic shift, light reverb for atmosphere. My Wave: tighter drum compression for meter readability, clearer mix for groove perception. The advisor should recognize that mix philosophy adapts not just per producer/engineer but per song.
mixing.comparison_to_fobd_mix: Same O'Brien but DIFFERENT mix priorities. FOBD's verse texture preserved with light reverb (atmosphere); My Wave's verse needs CLARITY (meter readability). FOBD's drums room-ambient; My Wave's drums close-mic-tight. Same engineer, two compositions, two mix approaches.

mastering.mastering_engineer_album: Stephen Marcussen at Marcussen Mastering (same as FOBD — full Superunknown album)
mastering.loudness_approach_documented: 1994 mid-loudness-war target preserved. Album-context mastering — My Wave fits with the album's overall loudness curve.
mastering.vinyl_vs_cd_documented: Original CD master by Marcussen. Vinyl release used different mastering pass.
mastering.tonal_balance_at_release: Similar to FOBD's tonal balance — bright top, present mids, controlled low end. Album-consistent mastering.

the_moment_people_remember.primary: The 5/4 GROOVE feeling NATURAL — listeners notice the unusual feel but rarely consciously identify it as 5/4. The groove is the song's identity.
the_moment_people_remember.secondary: The METER RESOLUTION at chorus — the shift from 5/4 verse to 4/4 chorus feels like physical relief, even when listeners don't analyze why.
the_moment_people_remember.tertiary: Cornell's PHRASING ADAPTATION to the 5/4 bar — vocal delivery that fits asymmetric meter without sounding awkward.

advisor_extract.description: Compose in 5/4 such that the meter FEELS natural (groovy) rather than awkward (academic). Achieved via: (1) drummer's confident pocket on the 5-beat bar (groupings 3+2 or 2+3 rather than 1+1+1+1+1 counting), (2) bass locked TIGHTLY with kick on the 5/4 pattern, (3) riff lands such that the 'extra beat' feels like a natural pickup rather than an interruption. Distinct from prog rock's deliberate 5/4 academic feel.
use_for: Songs wanting subtle metric sophistication that doesn't sound academic
compositions where odd meter should feel groovy and physical rather than mathematical
tracks targeting listeners who feel rather than count
advisor_extract.implementation_for_modern_compositions: In MIDI/sample context: set time signature to 5/4 for verse clip. Place kick + snare in groupings that feel natural (e.g., kick on beats 1 and 3, snare on 2 and 4 or beat 5 — varies per intent). The riff/lead must land such that the 5-beat completion feels like resolution, not interruption. Test by tapping foot to the groove — if it feels stilted, the 5/4 isn't working naturally.
advisor_extract.comparison_to_other_meter_recipes: 5/4 (My Wave) = groovy + playful. 6/4 (FOBD) = patient + introspective. 7/4 (Spoonman verse) = destabilizing but resolves to 4/4. 7/4 (Outshined chorus) = destabilizing as chorus is unstable. Each odd meter has different emotional default per direction (V→C resolution vs V→C escape).

advisor_extract.description: When composing in odd meter (5/4, 7/4, 6/4), drum patterns must use NATURAL groupings (e.g., 5/4 as 3+2 or 2+3, NOT 1+1+1+1+1 sequential). Natural groupings make the meter feel like a slightly extended 4/4 rather than a counting exercise. The drummer's confidence and pocket are critical — academic playing kills the groove.
use_for: Drum programming for odd-meter compositions
selecting drum patterns that make odd meter accessible
any rhythmic context where odd meter is used
advisor_extract.implementation_for_modern_compositions: When programming drum patterns in DAW: explicitly think about kick + snare placement in NATURAL pairs/triples within the bar. For 5/4 = pair (2 beats) + triple (3 beats). For 7/4 = pair + pair + triple, or pair + triple + pair. Avoid uniform spacing of hits — the asymmetry of the meter must be expressed in the pattern's grouping.

advisor_extract.description: When working with the same producer/engineer team across multiple songs on an album, mix decisions should ADAPT per song's compositional intent. FOBD wants moderate compression preserving dynamic shift + light reverb for atmosphere. My Wave wants tighter drum compression for meter readability + less reverb for clarity. Same team, different priorities per song's needs.
use_for: Album-context mixing where multiple songs need consistent overall character but distinct individual treatments
recognizing when a song needs different mix decisions than another song on the same album
advisor_extract.philosophical_note: The advisor should NOT default to 'same producer = same mix approach across album'. Mix philosophy adapts per song's compositional intent even within a single producer's body of work.

advisor_extract.description: When composing for a band with a versatile bassist, deploy the bassist DIFFERENTLY across different songs. Some songs showcase MELODIC bass (FOBD: Shepherd as melodically integrated). Other songs showcase RHYTHMIC PRECISION bass (My Wave: Shepherd locked tight with kick). Same player, two compositional faces.
use_for: Album arrangement decisions with capable bassists
ensuring bass role variety across an album
matching bass approach to song's compositional intent
advisor_extract.philosophical_note: Bass role is often defaulted to 'foundation locked with kick' regardless of song. Recognizing when bass should be MELODIC vs RHYTHMIC PRECISION is part of compositional sophistication.

advisor_extract.description: Within the same studio with the same producer, tracking decisions should prioritize different elements per song. Introspective songs prioritize vocal phrasing + clean texture clarity. Groove-driven songs prioritize kick-snare punch + bass-drum lock. The studio's resources adapt to the song's compositional intent.
use_for: Studio session planning when working on multiple songs
recognizing tracking priorities that match compositional needs
avoiding the default 'one-size-fits-all' tracking approach
advisor_extract.philosophical_note: Tracking priority is not just about producer's general approach but about per-song compositional needs. The advisor should help select what to capture cleanly vs what to leave atmospheric per song.

Overfloater.time_signature: 4/4 throughout — meter shifts not used here, departure from Superunknown signature device
Overfloater.track_runtime: ~6:42 — among the LONGEST Soundgarden album tracks. Extended atmospheric exploration over jam-band-influenced groove.
Overfloater._special_status: Late-career Soundgarden track. Down on the Upside (1996) was the band's last album before initial 1997 disbandment. Demonstrates the band's MATURE psychedelic side — extended jam form, atmospheric textures, less aggression, more rumination. Co-produced by Adam Kasper with the band itself.

composition.structural_blueprint: intro_atmospheric (clean guitar + cello-like sustained tones establishing mood) -> verse1_long (sustained vocal phrases, sparse instrumentation) -> chorus_subtle_lift -> verse2_long -> chorus -> EXTENDED_INSTRUMENTAL_BRIDGE (the song's structural centerpiece — multi-minute jam-influenced section) -> chorus_outro_extended -> long_fade. Total ~6:42, with extended bridge occupying significant portion.

section_count_and_lengths.intro_atmospheric: Extended ~8-12 bars establishing mood (longer than typical Soundgarden intros)
section_count_and_lengths.choruses: 8 bars but with subtle lift (not the explosion of FOBD chorus)

composition.harmonic_motion: Modal Aeolian with sustained chord textures. The verse uses slow chord changes (each chord held 2-4 bars), creating meditative harmonic pacing. Bridge introduces harmonic exploration via guitar work but stays modal. Drop D pedal tones underpin sustained voicings.
composition.melodic_contour: Verse vocal: SUSTAINED long phrases with subtle melodic motion — Cornell's most LANGUOROUS vocal style. Chorus: subtle ascending arc, less dramatic than typical Soundgarden chorus. Extended bridge: vocal absent, guitar work carries melody. Outro: returns to verse-style sustained phrases.
composition.characteristic_compositional_choice: EXTENDED FORM WITH MEDITATIVE ATMOSPHERE — the song trades typical rock structural economy for jam-band-influenced exploration. The extended bridge is the song's identity. Where JCP achieves length via sustained intensity and FOBD achieves length via traditional pop-rock structure, Overfloater achieves length via atmospheric exploration.
composition.harmonic_pacing: VERY SLOW — 2-4 bars per chord. The harmonic stability supports the song's meditative quality.
composition.phrase_symmetry: Variable phrase lengths — verses 12-16 bars rather than standard 8. The song's pacing breaks symmetry deliberately to create atmospheric flow.
composition.comparison_to_other_soundgarden_extended: JCP (5:50): extended via SUSTAINED INTENSITY. Overfloater (6:42): extended via ATMOSPHERIC EXPLORATION + JAM BRIDGE. Two different paths to extended form within the same band's catalog.

instrumentation_complete: electric guitar 1 (Kim Thayil — atmospheric textures + lead lines in bridge)
electric guitar 2 (Chris Cornell — sustained chord textures)
bass guitar (Ben Shepherd — melodic + foundational)
drum kit (Matt Cameron — restrained, jam-influenced playing)
lead vocal (Chris Cornell)
minimal documented overdubs beyond the band's live tracking + bridge guitar layering

section_instrumentation.intro_atmospheric: Sparse — clean/lightly distorted guitar + sustained tones, drums entering subtly, vocal absent
section_instrumentation.verse_1_long: Full band at LOW intensity — clean/lightly overdriven guitars, restrained drums, vocal exposed
section_instrumentation.chorus_subtle_lift: Density increases slightly but no aggressive shift — chorus is harmonic + melodic lift, not dynamic explosion
section_instrumentation.extended_instrumental_bridge: Vocal ABSENT for multiple minutes. Thayil's lead lines explore over rhythm section's continued atmospheric groove. The bridge is the song's structural showcase.
section_instrumentation.outro_chorus_extended: Returns to chorus harmonic content with vocal sustained phrasing, fades naturally

arrangement.vocal_layering_strategy: Mostly SINGLE-tracked Cornell — exposed and atmospheric. Possible subtle doubling on certain phrases but not the chorus-doubling-as-dynamic-lever of FOBD or HSB. The vocal serves the song's meditative quality.
arrangement.dynamic_arc_overall: FLAT-TO-MODERATE intensity. The song does NOT use dynamic peaks — verse and chorus differ slightly in density but both stay in low-to-moderate intensity zone. Extended bridge maintains this flat dynamic with instrumental variation.

instrumental_role_assignment.drums_role: Cameron RESTRAINED throughout — jam-band-influenced playing rather than rock kit aggression. Supports atmosphere over driving the song.
instrumental_role_assignment.guitar_role: Texture + atmosphere primary. Bridge: Thayil's lead work showcased as the song's melodic centerpiece (vocal absent in bridge so guitar carries melody).
instrumental_role_assignment.bass_role: Shepherd plays melodic + atmospheric — supports the song's meditative quality with subtle melodic motion under the sustained guitars.
instrumental_role_assignment.vocal_role: Languid sustained delivery — Cornell at his most introspective. Vocal absent for entire extended bridge.

harmonic_density_per_section.chorus: 5 instruments at MODERATE intensity (subtle lift, not explosion)
harmonic_density_per_section.extended_instrumental_bridge: 5 instruments minus vocal = 4 active. Density similar to verses but instrumental variation.

arrangement_anti_patterns_avoided_or_inv: Does NOT use dramatic loud-quiet-loud — flat-to-moderate intensity throughout
Does NOT use meter shift (departure from Superunknown signature device)
Does NOT use chorus-explosion dynamic — chorus is harmonic + melodic lift only
Does NOT use compact form (~3-4 min) — extends to 6:42 for atmospheric exploration
Does NOT center vocal as primary driver — vocal absent for extended bridge
arrangement.what_makes_arrangement_unique_within_sou: Overfloater represents Soundgarden's PSYCHEDELIC + JAM-INFLUENCED side. Where most Soundgarden tracks feature clear sectional dynamics + Cornell's vocal as primary, Overfloater features ATMOSPHERIC EXPLORATION + EXTENDED INSTRUMENTAL BRIDGE. The vocal-absent multi-minute middle is structurally bold for a 'rock band' format.
arrangement.comparison_to_jam_band_aesthetics: Overfloater shows Soundgarden's connection to extended-form rock traditions (Pink Floyd, Led Zeppelin's longer tracks). Where Cornell could write tight pop songs (Black Hole Sun), this song demonstrates the band's capacity for sustained atmospheric exploration — a different musical lineage.

performance.tempo_feel_description: Slow ~80 BPM with documented LANGUID feel — tempo lets every element breathe and sustain. Tracked LIVE per Down on the Upside's documented self-produced approach with Adam Kasper. The slow tempo demands precision (any drift exposed) but invites atmospheric playing rather than tight rhythmic precision.

drum_style.feel: Cameron's most JAM-INFLUENCED playing on the album. Restrained, atmospheric, supports the song's exploratory quality rather than driving it forward.
techniques_documented: Slow 4/4 with kick + snare backbeat at ballad-adjacent pace
Hi-hat 8ths or quarter notes — minimal articulation
Cymbal washes (slow swells, bell rolls) used texturally rather than as section markers
Bridge: drums maintain steady atmosphere through the extended instrumental — no fills as variation, just sustained groove
Tom hits sparse and intentional
drum_style.groove_feel_alignment: On beat, slight LAID-BACK feel matches the song's atmospheric character
drum_style.fills_and_transitions: Minimal — atmospheric continuity priority over section markers. The song's flow comes from sustained groove, not drum punctuation.

guitar_style.performer_thayil: Kim Thayil — atmospheric textures + bridge lead lines (the bridge is Thayil's compositional showcase)
guitar_style.performer_cornell: Chris Cornell — sustained chord textures supporting Thayil

feel_per_section.intro_atmospheric: Clean/lightly overdriven sustained tones, slow chord changes
feel_per_section.chorus: Slight density increase via guitar layering
feel_per_section.extended_bridge: Thayil's lead lines explore over rhythm section's continued atmosphere — multi-minute melodic guitar work
feel_per_section.outro: Returns to chorus texture, fades naturally

techniques_documented: Drop D tuning with sustained chord voicings
Atmospheric guitar approach — sustain + slight feedback used texturally
Bridge lead lines: melodic phrasing, not virtuosic shred — Thayil's documented style
Two-guitar layering for sustained chord thickness
guitar_style.playing_style_versus_other_soundgarden: Where JCP guitars provide aggressive distortion identity and FOBD guitars use clean-to-distorted dynamic shift, Overfloater guitars create ATMOSPHERIC TEXTURE that serves as the song's continuous fabric. Guitars as ATMOSPHERE rather than as RIFF or as DYNAMIC LEVER.

bass_performance.role: Atmospheric + melodic bass. Shepherd plays slow melodic root motion that supports the sustained guitars without rushing the harmonic pacing. The bass contributes to the song's meditative quality rather than driving rhythmic energy.

vocal_delivery.verse_delivery: LANGUOROUS — Cornell's most sustained, contemplative vocal style on the album. Phrases held longer than typical Soundgarden, breath-supported delivery without strain.
vocal_delivery.chorus_delivery: Subtle lift to upper-mid register, sustained held notes. Less dramatic than FOBD or JCP — the chorus is harmonic + melodic atmosphere, not explosion.
vocal_delivery.outro_delivery: Returns to verse-style sustained delivery, fades naturally with the song
documented_vocal_quirks: Cornell at his most ATMOSPHERIC vocal — different from his aggressive (JCP) or dynamic-shift (FOBD) approaches
Vocal absent for entire extended instrumental bridge — the song trusts the band to carry the listener through the bridge without vocal anchor
Multiple complete takes recorded; best chosen per Down on the Upside's documented approach

performance_anti_patterns_avoided: No quantization or pitch correction (preserves natural feel)
No vocal layering as dynamic device — single voice carries the atmospheric quality
No drum fills used as section markers — atmospheric continuity priority

recording.studio: Studio Litho, Seattle, Washington (Stone Gossard's studio — documented Down on the Upside primary tracking location)
recording.primary_engineer_producer: Adam Kasper (co-producer with Soundgarden — the album was famously SELF-PRODUCED with Kasper as engineer/co-producer rather than external producer-driven)
recording.subsequent_remix: Adam Kasper mixed (the band + Kasper handled mixing rather than separating producer + mix engineer roles like Beinhorn/O'Brien)

drum_micing_approach.philosophy_kasper: Multiple close mics + room mics. Studio Litho's tracking room captures kit with controlled ambience. Kasper's approach more BAND-DRIVEN than producer-imposed — the band had final say on tracking decisions.
specifics_for_overfloater: Slow tempo + restrained Cameron playing means room mics capture extensive natural decay
Drum sound NATURAL and breathing — closer to Albini's room emphasis than Date's compression-tracking
Light compression at tracking, but more dynamic range preserved than Beinhorn's Superunknown work

guitar_tracking_approach.philosophy_kasper_with_band: Two-guitar approach standard, but with EMPHASIS on TEXTURE over distortion punch
specifics_documented: Drop D tuned guitars
Sustained chord voicings recorded with priority on natural sustain — minimal compression at tracking
Bridge lead lines tracked with care for Thayil's atmospheric phrasing
Tube amps (Soundgarden Down on the Upside era options)

bass_tracking_approach.specifics: DI + amp blend per Studio Litho approach. Shepherd's melodic + atmospheric role required clarity for the slow harmonic motion to read.

vocal_tracking_approach.philosophy_kasper: Vocal isolation booth at Studio Litho, but with attention to natural feel preservation
specifics_documented: Cornell tracked in vocal booth, dynamic mic for high SPL capability
Sustained languid delivery captured cleanly — long held notes preserved without compression artifacts
Multiple complete takes selected (Kasper's documented approach matches Beinhorn's selection-not-comping philosophy)

notable_instruments_and_gear_documented: Tube amps (Soundgarden's documented Down on the Upside era options)
Studio Litho's console (Studio Litho documented as having Neve console in main room)
what_makes_kasper_recording_distinct: BAND-DRIVEN production approach — the band had final say on tracking decisions, Kasper as engineer/co-producer rather than authoritative producer
More NATURAL recording aesthetic than Beinhorn's Superunknown (which had more producer-shaping)
Light compression at tracking — preserves dynamic range for the song's atmospheric character
Engineer + mixer roles unified (Kasper handled both) — closer to Date's Badmotorfinger approach than Beinhorn/O'Brien split
recording.kasper_vs_beinhorn_for_soundgarden: Beinhorn (Superunknown 1994): more PRODUCER-DRIVEN, paired with O'Brien for separate mixing. Kasper (Down on the Upside 1996): more BAND-DRIVEN, handled both engineering + mixing. The shift from Beinhorn-to-Kasper across Soundgarden's catalog reflects the band's growing self-direction in their late period.
recording.production_philosophy_progression_across: DATE (1991 Badmotorfinger): tracker-mixer dual role, capture-focused, raw. BEINHORN (1994 Superunknown): producer-driven with separate O'Brien mixer, more shaped/polished. KASPER (1996 Down on the Upside): band-driven, engineer/co-producer dual role, natural + atmospheric. Three production approaches across three Soundgarden albums — the same band's relationship to producers evolved across their catalog.

mixing.mix_engineer: Adam Kasper (mixed at Studio Litho — engineer/co-producer/mixer ROLE UNIFIED, distinct from Beinhorn+O'Brien split on Superunknown)
mixing.mixing_philosophy_kasper: BAND-DRIVEN MIX. Kasper's documented approach favored preserving the band's tracking decisions rather than imposing external sonic shaping. Mixes prioritize NATURAL + ATMOSPHERIC over polished + punchy.
drum_mix_balance: Room mics PROMINENT in mix — natural ambience preserved, kit feels in-room rather than close-mic-stacked
Cameron's restrained playing means hi-hat + ride cymbal articulation is audible in mid-range rather than buried
Kick + snare punchy but NOT over-compressed — dynamic range preserved for the song's atmospheric character
Toms with natural decay — bridge sections allow drum tail to bloom
guitar_mix_treatment: Two guitars panned hard L/R for stereo width across the song's atmospheric spread
EQ EMPHASIZES upper-mid sustain (~1-3 kHz) for chord clarity over the slow harmonic motion
Less aggressive compression than Beinhorn's Superunknown — preserves natural sustain decay
Bridge lead guitar floats forward in mix briefly before retreating into texture — dynamic mix automation
vocal_mix_treatment: Cornell vocal centered, slightly forward but NOT crowding the atmospheric texture
Plate reverb + medium hall reverb blend — gives vocal SPACE matching the song's contemplative mood
Light compression preserves Cornell's sustained held notes (long phrases breathe naturally)
Vocal absent for entire extended bridge — mix opens up to instrumental texture without vocal anchor
bass_mix_treatment: Shepherd's bass clearly audible in low-mid (~120-300 Hz) — bass functions melodically not just rhythmically
Light compression preserves natural envelope of plucked notes
Bass + kick relationship: complementary frequency placement (kick fundamentals ~60Hz, bass fundamentals ~80-120Hz)
spatial_treatment_atmosphere: Reverb usage MORE than typical Soundgarden — the song's atmospheric quality requires spatial depth
Plate reverbs on guitars + drums provide 'room' feel without artificial-sounding hall ambience
Bridge section uses MORE reverb than verses — spatial expansion mirrors structural opening up
Stereo field is WIDE (guitars hard L/R) but vocals + bass + kick anchor center for solidity
automation_used_in_mix: Vocal level rides — Cornell's dynamic phrases compensated for via mix automation rather than over-compression
Bridge instrument level rides — guitars + drums lift slightly during bridge, then retreat for outro
Reverb send automation — bridge has more reverb, verses tighter for vocal clarity
mix_anti_patterns_avoided: No hyper-compression (preserves dynamic range that the song's atmospheric character requires)
No drum sample replacement (acoustic kit sound preserved)
No vocal pitch correction or layering as dynamic device
No genre-typical 'rock loudness' aesthetic that would compress the song's natural breathing
mixing.comparison_to_jcp_mix: JCP mix is COMPRESSED + INTENSE + tight stereo for sustained pressure. Overfloater mix is OPEN + ATMOSPHERIC + wide spatial spread for contemplative breathing. Same band, same engineer-class (Date/Kasper both engineer-mixer hybrids), but mix philosophy serves the song's compositional intent.
mixing.comparison_to_fobd_mix: FOBD mix (Beinhorn + O'Brien) is dynamic-shift-driven with quiet/loud contrast as mix axis. Overfloater mix (Kasper unified) is atmospheric continuity with subtle structural lifts. Different mix philosophies for different compositional intents.
mixing.what_makes_kasper_mix_distinct_in_catalo: Where Beinhorn+O'Brien polished Superunknown for radio impact, Kasper mixed Down on the Upside for BAND AUTHENTICITY. The mix sounds closer to how the band sounded in the room — less sonic shaping, more capture-fidelity. This is a band-late-period choice valuing AUTHENTICITY over MARKET-OPTIMIZATION.

mastering.mastering_engineer_album: Down on the Upside (1996) mastered by Stephen Marcussen (documented as continuing Soundgarden's Marcussen-mastered catalog from Superunknown)
mastering_philosophy_for_overfloater: Marcussen's documented approach: high-end clarity + low-end weight + preserves dynamics
1996 album-level mastering — Down on the Upside SLIGHTLY pre-loudness-war, preserves more dynamic range than late-90s/early-2000s rock masters
Overfloater's atmospheric character requires preserving dynamic range — mastering does not over-compress
mastering.loudness_observed: Album average around -10 to -11 LUFS integrated estimate (1996 mastering, pre-aggressive-loudness-war). Overfloater specifically slightly quieter than album peak tracks.
mastering.spectral_signature: Marcussen's documented sound: present mid-range, smooth top end, tight low end. Less hyped than mid-2000s rock mastering.
what_mastering_preserves_for_overfloater: Natural drum decay (kick + snare envelopes preserved)
Sustained guitar texture clarity in the long bridge section
Cornell's dynamic vocal phrasing (long held notes preserved)

the_moment_people_remember.level_1_signature_moment: THE EXTENDED INSTRUMENTAL BRIDGE (~3:00 onward) — multi-minute vocal-absent atmospheric exploration. Listeners remember the bridge as the song's identity, not the verses or choruses.
the_moment_people_remember.level_2_arrangement_signature: JAM-BAND-INFLUENCED EXTENDED FORM in a hard-rock context. Soundgarden trades typical rock structural economy for sustained atmospheric exploration — psychedelic + meditative feel rare in their catalog.
the_moment_people_remember.level_3_compositional_signature: EXTENDED FORM (6:42) achieved via ATMOSPHERIC EXPLORATION rather than via SUSTAINED INTENSITY (JCP) or via TRADITIONAL POP-ROCK STRUCTURE (FOBD). A third path to long-form rock writing within the same band's catalog. Demonstrates that LENGTH IS A COMPOSITIONAL CHOICE that must serve a specific function — Overfloater's length serves contemplative immersion.

advisor_extract.applicability: Long-form rock/post-rock/psychedelic compositions where the listener should be carried through extended instrumental exploration. Use when the SONG'S IDENTITY is the bridge, not the verses/choruses.

core_compositional_recipes.rule: Build a 6+ minute rock track around an EXTENDED MIDDLE SECTION (3+ minutes of instrumental exploration). Verses + choruses become FRAMING for the central instrumental statement. Vocal absent for substantial duration trusts the band to carry listener attention via texture + harmonic motion.
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Overfloater (Soundgarden, Down on the Upside 1996, 6:42) — extended instrumental bridge is the song's identity
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Requires excellent musicians + atmospheric instrumental writing. Won't work in radio-format contexts.
core_compositional_recipes.personality_fit: Yorke 50 / Cobain 20 / Reznor 15 / Daft Punk 15 (Yorke for atmospheric trust + length tolerance)

core_compositional_recipes.rule: Make the BRIDGE the song's structural center (longest section, peak moment) rather than treating it as a transition. Bridge length should EXCEED chorus or verse length combined. Bridge harmonic motion should DIVERGE from verse/chorus pattern (modal, suspended, exploratory).
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Overfloater extended bridge — multiple minutes long, harmonically distinct from verse/chorus
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Requires bridge to STAND on its own as compelling music — can't just be 'the part between sections'

core_compositional_recipes.rule: Borrow extended-form rock traditions (Pink Floyd, Led Zeppelin's longer tracks, Phish-adjacent jam sensibility) into a hard-rock or alt-rock context. Allow space for instrumental exploration without rushing back to vocal sections. The BAND's collective improvisation/arrangement carries the listener.
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Overfloater bridge — jam-band-influenced extended texture in a hard-rock catalog
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Genre-crossing; risk alienating listeners expecting tight rock economy

core_compositional_recipes.rule: Have NO VOCAL for an extended section (2+ minutes) in a vocal-driven song. The instrumental texture must be compelling enough to hold attention without lyrical anchor. Used as DRAMATIC choice — the absence of voice becomes a structural statement.
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Overfloater multi-minute vocal-absent bridge — listener trusts band to carry them through
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Listeners conditioned to expect vocal hooks may disengage. Requires confident instrumental writing.

core_compositional_recipes.rule: Use UNIFIED engineering + mixing roles (one person tracks AND mixes) to preserve recording-stage decisions through to final master. Avoid the producer-driven external-mixer split when the band's tracking aesthetic IS the desired final aesthetic. Result: more authentic, less sonically-shaped final product.
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Adam Kasper engineered + co-produced + mixed Down on the Upside (1996) — band-driven, engineer-mixer unified role
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Less external sonic shaping; less radio-optimized; more authenticity-leaning. Requires excellent tracking decisions upfront.

core_compositional_recipes.rule: Build a song where SUSTAINED ATMOSPHERIC CONTINUITY is the primary aesthetic, NOT quiet/loud dynamic contrast. Sections shift via harmonic + textural variation rather than via volume + intensity changes. Reverb + sustain + spacious mix support this.
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Overfloater — atmospheric continuity across 6:42, no quiet/loud dynamic contrast as structural device
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Less obvious 'rock dynamics' for listeners conditioned to verse/chorus contrast. Requires harmonic + textural interest to sustain.

production_philosophy_signature_kasper.philosophy: Band-driven engineer-mixer unified role. Producer/engineer/mixer in ONE person, working WITH the band rather than over them. Preserves tracking decisions, avoids over-shaping in mix. Authenticity-leaning.
production_philosophy_signature_kasper.applicability: Late-career bands who know their sound, capture-focused projects, projects where authenticity matters more than commercial polish.
production_philosophy_signature_kasper.documented_lineage: Adam Kasper at Studio Litho — Soundgarden Down on the Upside, Pearl Jam Yield-era work. Distinct from Beinhorn+O'Brien producer-mixer split + from Date's tracker-mixer dual role within external-producer paradigm.

advisor_extract.structural_choice_summary: Extended atmospheric form, jam bridge as centerpiece, vocal-absent extended section, atmospheric continuity over dynamic contrast — Overfloater demonstrates Soundgarden's MATURE psychedelic + jam-influenced side, a structurally bold late-period choice within their catalog.

Head_Down.time_signature: Predominantly 7/4 (documented odd-meter — different from My Wave's 5/4 lilt; 7/4 creates a longer asymmetric cycle)
Head_Down.tuning_documented: Drop D + flat tunings explored on Superunknown — Head Down operates with low drone-friendly tuning
Head_Down._special_status: Co-written by Ben Shepherd (bassist) — credited with substantial input on Head Down's compositional structure. Demonstrates Soundgarden's collective writing approach on Superunknown where multiple members contributed primary writing credits. The 7/4 time signature, drone-bass approach, and atmospheric textures are documented as Shepherd-driven compositional choices.

composition.primary_compositional_signature: ASYMMETRIC 7/4 METER WITH SHEPHERD-DRIVEN DRONE BASS — the song's identity is its long 7/4 cycle (different from 4/4 or 5/4) combined with a sustained low-drone foundation. Where My Wave uses 5/4 as playful lilt, Head Down uses 7/4 as STATELY + HYPNOTIC structural device.
composition.core_structural_arc: Slow drone-build intro → 7/4 verse establishing drone modal motion → atmospheric chorus expanding harmonic field → instrumental bridge with sustained drone foundation → return to verse → extended outro with drone resolution. Total ~6:08, atmospheric long-form similar to Overfloater but with DIFFERENT compositional engine (asymmetric meter + drone vs. atmospheric jam exploration).
composition.harmonic_motion_summary: MODAL + DRONE-CENTERED. Tonic B minor area as drone anchor; harmonic motion happens via SUSPENDED VOICINGS over sustained low B foundation rather than via traditional chord progression. Aeolian + occasional Phrygian inflection. The drone is the song's harmonic gravity well.
composition.characteristic_compositional_choice: DRONE-AS-COMPOSITIONAL-FOUNDATION — Shepherd's bass establishes a sustained low-frequency anchor that all other harmonic motion happens RELATIVE TO. This differs from typical rock harmonic motion (chord-to-chord progression) and aligns more with raga or drone-doom traditions.
composition.comparison_to_my_wave_odd_meter: My Wave uses 5/4 as PLAYFUL LILT — meter as groove device. Head Down uses 7/4 as STATELY HYPNOTIC CYCLE — meter as structural device. Both are odd-meter Soundgarden tracks but with totally different compositional intents: My Wave is groove-driven, Head Down is meditation-driven.
composition.comparison_to_overfloater_atmospheric: Overfloater achieves atmospheric quality via JAM-BAND extended form + extended bridge. Head Down achieves atmospheric quality via DRONE-CENTERED HARMONY + asymmetric meter. Two paths to atmospheric long-form Soundgarden writing.
composition.compositional_economy: Head Down's 6:08 length is justified by HARMONIC MEDITATION on the drone — listeners are invited to inhabit the harmonic space. Compositional 'movement' is subtle (suspended voicing shifts, modal inflections) rather than dramatic.
composition.innovation_for_genre_context: 1994 mainstream rock context: most singles/album tracks were 3-5 min in straightforward 4/4 with verse-chorus pop structure. Head Down at 6:08 in 7/4 with drone-modal harmony was COMPOSITIONALLY ADVENTUROUS for its commercial context (Superunknown sold 5M+ copies in US — this song reached that audience).

arrangement.primary_arrangement_signature: DRONE-FOUNDATION + ASYMMETRIC METER — bass holds sustained low drone, drums establish 7/4 cycle, guitars provide atmospheric harmonic texture above the drone, vocal floats over the architecture.
arrangement.intro_arrangement: Slow drone-build — bass establishes low foundation, drums enter with 7/4 cycle, guitars layer atmospheric texture. Several measures of meter establishment before vocal entry.
arrangement.verse_arrangement: Bass DRONE on tonic B (sustained low frequency anchor). Drums in 7/4 (kick/snare pattern adapted to asymmetric cycle). Two guitars: one rhythm with sustained chord voicings, one atmospheric texture. Cornell vocal sustained-melodic over the drone foundation.
arrangement.chorus_arrangement: Slight harmonic expansion — chord motion ABOVE the drone (drone remains constant) rather than full chord progression. Cornell vocal lifts to upper mid register. Drums maintain 7/4 cycle.
arrangement.bridge_arrangement: Extended instrumental section with INTENSIFIED drone (bass + low guitars together emphasize the low foundation). Atmospheric guitar leads/textures above. Drums maintain 7/4 throughout.
arrangement.outro_arrangement: Return to verse-style arrangement with extended fade — drone resolves slowly, atmospheric texture continues until song fades.
arrangement_anti_patterns_avoided: No traditional verse-chorus dynamic contrast (atmospheric continuity)
No drum sample replacement or loops
arrangement.what_makes_arrangement_unique_within_sou: Head Down represents Soundgarden's DRONE-MODAL + ASYMMETRIC METER side. Where most Soundgarden tracks operate within 4/4 rock paradigm (or 5/4 playful), Head Down inhabits a meditation-music harmonic space within rock instrumentation. Closest to Black Sabbath's drone-doom influence but with Cornell's melodic vocal sensibility above.
arrangement.comparison_to_jcp_arrangement: JCP arrangement is RIFF-DRIVEN sustained intensity. Head Down arrangement is DRONE-DRIVEN sustained meditation. Two opposite arrangement aesthetics within same band's catalog.
arrangement.comparison_to_fobd_arrangement: FOBD arrangement is QUIET-LOUD DYNAMIC CONTRAST verse-chorus pop-rock. Head Down arrangement is ATMOSPHERIC CONTINUITY drone-modal meditation. Different arrangement engines.
arrangement.comparison_to_my_wave_arrangement: My Wave arrangement is GROOVE-DRIVEN 5/4 playful + harmonic motion. Head Down arrangement is DRONE-FOUNDATION 7/4 hypnotic + harmonic stasis. Both odd-meter but opposite arrangement philosophies.
arrangement.comparison_to_overfloater_arrangement: Overfloater arrangement is JAM-BAND atmospheric + extended bridge as centerpiece. Head Down arrangement is DRONE-MODAL meditation + asymmetric meter as structural anchor. Different paths to long-form atmospheric Soundgarden.

performance.tempo_feel_description: STATELY HYPNOTIC — 86-92 BPM in 7/4 cycle creates a long asymmetric pulse that feels meditative rather than driving. The 7-beat cycle is long enough to feel SPACIOUS rather than urgent.

drum_style.approach: ASYMMETRIC METER MASTERY — Cameron documented as one of rock's most fluent odd-meter drummers. Head Down 7/4 played with confidence + groove rather than counted-stiffly.
specifics_documented: 7/4 cycle subdivided naturally — Cameron's documented approach to odd meters: feel the long cycle, place kicks/snares/cymbals to articulate the asymmetric pulse
Restrained intensity — not driving the song forward but ANCHORING the drone foundation
Hi-hat + ride cymbal articulation prominent in mid-range
Minimal fills — fills would interrupt the meditative cycle
drum_style.comparison_to_my_wave_drumming: My Wave Cameron drums establish 5/4 GROOVE FEEL playfully. Head Down Cameron drums establish 7/4 STATELY CYCLE meditatively. Same drummer, two opposite odd-meter approaches.
drum_style.comparison_to_overfloater_drumming: Overfloater Cameron drums establish atmospheric pulse with subtle dynamics. Head Down Cameron drums anchor 7/4 cycle with hypnotic regularity. Both atmospheric Soundgarden tracks but with different drum-arrangement intents.

guitar_style.rhythm_guitar_role: SUSTAINED HARMONIC TEXTURE above the drone foundation — chord voicings held for long durations, minimal palm-muting or rhythmic chunking
guitar_style.atmospheric_guitar_role: Layered sustained chord voicings + occasional atmospheric leads — guitars provide harmonic FIELD rather than rhythmic engine
specifics_documented: Drop D + flat tunings (Soundgarden Superunknown era exploration of low-tunings)
Distortion with sustain emphasis — long held chords with controlled feedback
Voicings often suspended or modal (sus2, sus4, modal voicings) — preserve the drone-friendly harmonic openness
Lead guitar atmospheric phrases in bridge sections
guitar_style.playing_style_versus_other_soundgarden: Where JCP guitars provide sustained intensity riff, FOBD guitars use clean-to-distorted dynamic shift, My Wave guitars groove in 5/4, Overfloater guitars create atmospheric texture, Head Down guitars provide DRONE-FRIENDLY HARMONIC FIELD above sustained low foundation. Five distinct guitar approaches across five Soundgarden songs in this dissection.

bass_performance.approach: DRONE-FOUNDATION + COMPOSITIONAL DRIVER — Shepherd co-wrote the song and his bass drone is its compositional anchor. Sustained low B notes form the harmonic gravity well
specifics_documented: Drop D / flat-tuned bass for low frequency drone capability
Sustained held notes — bass functions as drone instrument NOT as walking/rhythmic bass
Subtle melodic phrases between sustained passages
Bass + low-tuned guitars together emphasize the low-frequency foundation
bass_performance.shepherd_compositional_role: Shepherd's documented Superunknown contributions include Head Down (co-write) — demonstrates Soundgarden's collective writing approach where bassist could drive a song's compositional identity, not just support a guitarist's riff.

vocal_delivery.verse_delivery: FLOATING + SUSTAINED — Cornell's vocal hovers above the drone foundation rather than driving the song forward. Long held phrases, breath-supported delivery, less aggressive than JCP or even FOBD chorus
vocal_delivery.chorus_delivery: Slight upper-register lift but still atmospheric — chorus is harmonic + melodic field, not explosion
vocal_delivery.bridge_delivery: Vocal less prominent in bridge — instrumental drone foundation carries the section
documented_vocal_quirks: Cornell adapts to atmospheric drone context — more SUSTAINED than ARGUMENTATIVE
Long phrasing matches the long 7/4 cycle — vocal phrases align with structural cycle
Less wide dynamic range than other Superunknown vocals — Head Down requires consistent atmospheric vocal

performance_anti_patterns_avoided: No quantization or pitch correction (preserves natural feel)
No vocal layering as dynamic device — single voice carries the atmospheric quality

recording.studio: Bad Animals Studio, Seattle, Washington (Superunknown primary tracking — Studio X / Bad Animals)
recording.primary_producer: Michael Beinhorn (producer for Superunknown — same producer as JCP and FOBD)
recording.primary_mix_engineer: Brendan O'Brien (mixer for Superunknown — producer-mixer SPLIT, Beinhorn produced + O'Brien mixed)

drum_micing_approach.philosophy_beinhorn: Multiple close mics + room mics. Bad Animals tracking room captures kit with controlled ambience. Beinhorn's producer-driven approach shapes the kit sound at tracking
specifics_for_head_down: Slow tempo + restrained Cameron playing means room mics capture extensive natural decay
7/4 cycle articulation requires clear kick + snare + cymbal balance for listener to track the asymmetric pulse
Light compression at tracking, but preserved dynamic range for atmospheric character

guitar_tracking_approach.philosophy_beinhorn: Two-guitar approach with EMPHASIS on sustained texture for Head Down's drone-friendly arrangement
specifics_documented: Drop D + flat tunings
Sustained chord voicings recorded with priority on natural sustain decay
Tube amps (Soundgarden Superunknown era options)
Feedback-friendly tracking — sustained chords benefit from controlled feedback that mics capture

bass_tracking_approach.specifics: DI + amp blend per Bad Animals approach. Drone-foundation role required EXTRA care for low-frequency clarity — sustained low B notes need to read clearly without muddying the mix

vocal_tracking_approach.philosophy_beinhorn: Vocal isolation booth at Bad Animals, multiple complete takes selected (Beinhorn's documented selection-not-comping philosophy)
specifics_documented: Cornell tracked in vocal booth, dynamic mic for high SPL capability
Atmospheric sustained delivery captured cleanly — long held phrases preserved without compression artifacts
Less vocal layering than typical mid-90s rock — single voice priority for atmospheric coherence

notable_instruments_and_gear_documented: Bad Animals' SSL console + outboard gear
what_makes_beinhorn_recording_distinct_f: Producer-driven shaping at tracking — Beinhorn shaped the drone-foundation aesthetic for radio-compatible coherence even within atmospheric arrangement
Tracking + mixing roles SPLIT (Beinhorn produced + tracked, O'Brien mixed) — different from Date's unified or Kasper's unified roles
Compression at tracking, but Head Down's atmospheric character preserves more dynamic range than Superunknown peak intensity tracks (JCP-equivalents)
Multiple complete vocal takes selected per Beinhorn's documented preference
recording.comparison_to_my_wave_recording: Same album (Superunknown), same producer (Beinhorn), same studio (Bad Animals), same mixer (O'Brien), but DIFFERENT recording emphasis: My Wave tracked for 5/4 groove clarity, Head Down tracked for 7/4 drone foundation. Different songs require different tracking-stage decisions even within the same album session.
recording.comparison_to_overfloater_recording: Overfloater (1996, Studio Litho, Kasper unified role) vs. Head Down (1993, Bad Animals, Beinhorn+O'Brien split). Both atmospheric Soundgarden tracks but with TOTALLY different production approaches: Kasper band-driven authenticity vs. Beinhorn producer-driven shaping. Same band's atmospheric capacity expressed through different production frames.

mixing.mix_engineer: Brendan O'Brien (Superunknown mixed throughout — same mixer as JCP, FOBD, My Wave on this album)
mixing.mixing_studio: Southern Tracks Recording, Atlanta, Georgia (O'Brien's documented Superunknown mix base)
mixing.mixing_philosophy_obrien_for_head_down: DRONE-FOUNDATION CLARITY + ATMOSPHERIC SPACE. O'Brien's documented Superunknown approach adapted to Head Down's drone-modal compositional intent rather than imposing radio-rock template. Mix prioritizes LOW-FREQUENCY clarity (the drone) + WIDE STEREO ATMOSPHERE (the harmonic field above the drone).
drum_mix_balance: Kick + bass-low coordination CRITICAL — kick fundamentals (~60Hz) must complement Shepherd's drone (~80-120Hz fundamentals) without muddying
Snare slightly more present than typical atmospheric mix — needed to articulate the 7/4 cycle for listener comprehension
Cymbals (hi-hat + ride) prominent in upper mid (~2-5 kHz) for cycle articulation
Room mics blended to give kit natural breathing space
guitar_mix_treatment: Two guitars panned hard L/R for stereo width across the atmospheric harmonic field
EQ EMPHASIZES upper-mid sustain (~1-3 kHz) for chord clarity over the drone
Less aggressive compression than JCP mix — sustained drone-friendly textures need natural decay preservation
Bridge atmospheric leads float forward briefly, retreat into texture — mix dynamic automation
vocal_mix_treatment: Cornell vocal centered, slightly forward — needs to read clearly above the drone foundation
Plate reverb + medium hall reverb blend gives vocal SPACE matching atmospheric mood
Light compression preserves Cornell's sustained held phrases
EQ carve in low-mid (~200-400Hz) avoids vocal clashing with drone bass
bass_mix_treatment: Shepherd's bass drone is COMPOSITIONALLY CENTRAL — mix gives it MORE presence than typical bass placement
Low-frequency clarity priority — sustained low B notes must read clearly without becoming a wall of low rumble
Light compression preserves natural envelope; bass + kick coordinated via complementary frequency placement
Bass SLIGHTLY more forward than JCP/FOBD/My Wave bass mix because the drone IS the foundation
spatial_treatment_drone_atmosphere: Reverb usage MORE than typical Superunknown tracks — drone-modal harmonic field benefits from spatial depth
Plate reverbs on guitars + drums provide 'room' feel without artificial-sounding hall ambience
Bridge section uses MORE reverb than verses — spatial expansion mirrors structural opening up
Stereo field WIDE (guitars hard L/R) but bass + kick + vocal anchor center for solidity
automation_used_in_mix: Vocal level rides — Cornell's atmospheric phrasing compensated for via mix automation
Bridge instrument level rides — guitars + drums lift slightly during bridge, then retreat for outro
Reverb send automation — bridge has more reverb, verses tighter for vocal clarity
Bass level CONSISTENT — drone foundation should not waver
mix_anti_patterns_avoided: No over-compression of drone bass (would destroy the song's foundational character)
No vocal pitch correction or layering as dynamic device
No 4/4-style pop-rock mix template imposed (the 7/4 asymmetric cycle dictates different mix priorities)
mixing.comparison_to_my_wave_mix: Same album, same mixer (O'Brien). My Wave mix highlights 5/4 GROOVE PLAYFULNESS — kick/snare punch, mid-range guitar bite. Head Down mix highlights 7/4 DRONE FOUNDATION — bass presence, atmospheric reverb, low-frequency clarity. O'Brien adapts mix philosophy to compositional intent within the same album session.
mixing.comparison_to_overfloater_mix: Overfloater mix (Kasper unified role, Studio Litho, band-driven) is atmospheric continuity with subtle structural lifts. Head Down mix (O'Brien, Southern Tracks, producer-mixer-split) is drone-foundation with asymmetric cycle clarity. Both atmospheric Soundgarden but with DIFFERENT compositional engines requiring DIFFERENT mix priorities.

mastering.mastering_engineer_album: Stephen Marcussen (mastered Superunknown — documented album-level mastering engineer; same engineer as Down on the Upside establishing Soundgarden-Marcussen continuity)
mastering_philosophy_for_head_down: Marcussen's documented approach: high-end clarity + low-end weight + preserves dynamics
Head Down's drone foundation requires PRESERVED LOW-END WEIGHT — mastering must not over-compress the bass drone or it loses its foundational character
1994 album-level mastering — Superunknown SLIGHTLY pre-loudness-war but already commercially-loud-enough for radio impact (5M+ US sales)
mastering.loudness_observed: Album average around -10 to -11 LUFS integrated estimate. Head Down specifically slightly quieter than album peak tracks (Spoonman, Black Hole Sun) due to atmospheric compositional intent.
mastering.spectral_signature: Marcussen's documented sound: present mid-range, smooth top end, tight low end. Head Down benefits from this approach — the drone reads clearly without becoming muddy.
what_mastering_preserves_for_head_down: Low-frequency drone clarity (Shepherd's bass + low-tuned guitars)
Sustained guitar texture clarity in atmospheric sections

the_moment_people_remember.level_1_signature_moment: THE 7/4 ASYMMETRIC CYCLE OVER DRONE — listeners feel the long asymmetric pulse over Shepherd's sustained low foundation. The cycle creates a HYPNOTIC + STATELY feel rare in mainstream rock context.
the_moment_people_remember.level_2_arrangement_signature: DRONE-FOUNDATION + ASYMMETRIC METER. Bass holds sustained low drone, drums establish 7/4 cycle, guitars provide harmonic field above the drone, vocal floats. A meditation-music compositional engine within rock instrumentation.
the_moment_people_remember.level_3_compositional_signature: ASYMMETRIC METER + DRONE-CENTERED HARMONY as compositional foundation in 1994 commercial rock context. Demonstrates that ODD METER + DRONE COMPOSITIONAL ENGINE can succeed commercially when paired with strong atmospheric arrangement + competent musicianship. Shepherd's documented co-write demonstrates collective writing — bassist driving compositional identity, not just supporting guitar riffs.

advisor_extract.applicability: Atmospheric rock/post-rock/drone-doom-adjacent compositions where asymmetric meter + drone harmony serve meditative or hypnotic intent. Use when the SONG'S IDENTITY is the long cycle over the drone, not the harmonic motion.

core_compositional_recipes.rule: Use 7/4 (or other long asymmetric meters: 11/8, 13/8) as STRUCTURAL DEVICE rather than as decorative complexity. The long asymmetric cycle should feel HYPNOTIC + STATELY — listeners inhabit the cycle. 7-beat groupings can subdivide as 4+3, 3+4, 3+2+2, or 2+3+2 depending on rhythmic emphasis.
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Head Down (Soundgarden, Superunknown 1994, 7/4 throughout) — Cameron's 7/4 mastery makes it feel natural rather than arithmetic
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Requires excellent drummer + bassist to make asymmetric meter FEEL natural rather than counted-stiffly. Listeners conditioned to 4/4 may need time to lock in.
core_compositional_recipes.personality_fit: Yorke 35 / Cobain 25 / Reznor 15 / Daft Punk 25 (Yorke for asymmetric meter tolerance, Daft Punk for cyclic hypnotic quality)

core_compositional_recipes.rule: Build a song where SUSTAINED LOW-FREQUENCY DRONE is the harmonic gravity well. All other harmonic motion happens ABOVE the drone (suspended voicings, modal inflections) rather than via traditional chord progression. Drone should be present 80%+ of song duration.
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Head Down — Shepherd's sustained low B drone foundation across 6:08
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Requires excellent bass tone + sustain capability. May feel monotonous to listeners expecting harmonic motion.
core_compositional_recipes.personality_fit: Yorke 30 / Cobain 25 / Reznor 30 / Daft Punk 15 (Reznor for drone-doom influence, Yorke for atmospheric tolerance)

core_compositional_recipes.rule: Instead of standard chord progression (I-IV-V-I or i-VI-VII-i), use SUSTAINED MODAL VOICINGS above a drone foundation. Suspensions (sus2, sus4), modal inflections (Aeolian, Phrygian), and quartal/quintal voicings preserve harmonic openness while creating subtle motion.
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Head Down — modal voicings above drone foundation, harmonic motion via voicing shifts not chord changes
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Less obvious 'songwriting' for listeners conditioned to hook-driven progressions. Requires harmonic ear to write convincingly.

core_compositional_recipes.rule: Allow the BASSIST to drive primary compositional identity (riff, structure, harmonic foundation) rather than always centering guitar riffs. Documented as Shepherd's role on Head Down — the bass drone IS the compositional engine.
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Head Down — Ben Shepherd co-wrote, bass drone foundation is compositional anchor
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Requires bassist with compositional vision; band must trust bassist's structural ideas

core_compositional_recipes.rule: When using asymmetric meter (7/4, 5/4, 11/8), let the cycle feel LONG + MEDITATIVE rather than tight + complex. Slow tempos (80-95 BPM) + sustained instrumentation help listeners INHABIT the cycle rather than count it. Asymmetric meter as MEDITATIVE STRUCTURAL DEVICE not as virtuosic flex.
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Head Down 7/4 at ~88 BPM — meditative cycle, not virtuosic complexity
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Requires aesthetic restraint — temptation to show off odd-meter playing must be resisted

core_compositional_recipes.rule: When mixing drone-foundation arrangements, give the drone bass + low-tuned instruments DEDICATED low-frequency space. Coordinate kick (~60Hz) + bass drone (~80-120Hz) via complementary frequency placement. Avoid over-compressing the drone — preserve its natural envelope. Light dedicated reverb on drone-frequency layers can enhance spatial depth.
core_compositional_recipes.documented_example: Head Down O'Brien mix — bass drone clearly audible without becoming muddy
core_compositional_recipes.trade_offs: Requires careful frequency carving + restraint with compression

production_philosophy_signature_obrien_a.philosophy: Producer-mixer split (Beinhorn produced, O'Brien mixed) ADAPTED to drone-foundation compositional intent. Mix priorities shift from radio-rock template to drone-clarity + asymmetric-cycle articulation.
production_philosophy_signature_obrien_a.applicability: Atmospheric arrangements requiring low-frequency clarity + asymmetric cycle articulation within commercial production framework
