two_guitar_stereo_separation_distinct_pl.applicability_text: Two-guitar bands wanting to highlight player distinctness; songs where left/right interplay is part of the composition; tracks where stereo is a compositional axis not just a mix decision.
two_guitar_stereo_separation_distinct_pl.trade_offs: Demands two distinct guitar players + gear setups. Mono-compatibility risk. Less stereo impact than wall-of-sound multi-layered approach.
two_guitar_stereo_separation_distinct_pl.documented_example: JCP — Kim Thayil + Chris Cornell's two-guitar L/R separation, distinct gear voicings audible per side.

extended_aggressive_track_5_min_plus.rule: Sustain aggressive intensity across 5+ minutes — the LENGTH itself becomes part of the aggressive statement. Listener experiences sustained pressure as compositional choice, not endurance test. Demands material strong enough to bear extended runtime without monotony.
extended_aggressive_track_5_min_plus.applicability_text: Album tracks where aggressive identity is paramount; songs targeting Reznor/industrial sustained-intensity territory; tracks where the band must demonstrate stamina + commitment.
extended_aggressive_track_5_min_plus.trade_offs: Demands material strong enough to bear 5+ min sustained intensity. Risk of monotony if dynamic variation insufficient. Won't suit radio-singles.
extended_aggressive_track_5_min_plus.documented_example: Jesus Christ Pose at ~5:50 — sustained max-intensity across full runtime; length as compositional statement.

vocal_high_range_exploitation_as_signatu.rule: Build verse + chorus melody around the vocalist's HIGH RANGE (upper tessitura). The high-range strain becomes the song's signature element — listener associates the track with the vocalist's ceiling-pushing delivery. Different from falsetto: this is full-voice high-range, not head-voice.
vocal_high_range_exploitation_as_signatu.applicability_text: Songs centered on vocal acrobatics; tracks where vocal performance IS the compositional statement; vocalists with documented high-range capacity (Cornell, Plant, Tate territory).
vocal_high_range_exploitation_as_signatu.trade_offs: Demands vocalist with high-range capacity + sustainable strain. Won't suit baritone or limited-range vocalists.
vocal_high_range_exploitation_as_signatu.documented_example: JCP — Cornell's high-range full-voice strain across the song's full runtime as signature element.

date_compression_balance_for_long_aggres.rule: When tracking + mixing extended-form aggressive material, use MODERATE compression rather than maximum — preserve dynamic detail across 5+ minutes so the aggression doesn't become flat. Terry Date's documented Badmotorfinger approach: compression for impact, but with dynamic headroom for the song's shape to read.
date_compression_balance_for_long_aggres.applicability_text: Long aggressive tracks needing dynamic preservation; mixes where over-compression would flatten the song's internal pacing; tracker-mixer dual-role workflows.
date_compression_balance_for_long_aggres.trade_offs: Less radio-impact than maximum compression. Demands mix engineer with dynamic-restraint capacity.
date_compression_balance_for_long_aggres.documented_example: Terry Date's mix of JCP — moderate compression preserving the song's internal dynamic shape across 5:50.

meter_shift_6_4_verse_to_4_4_chorus.rule: Compose verse in 6/4 (or other asymmetric/extended meter) and chorus in 4/4 (or vice versa). Meter SHIFT between sections marks structural change without explicit announcement. Listener feels the section change rhythmically before recognizing it intellectually.
meter_shift_6_4_verse_to_4_4_chorus.applicability_text: Songs wanting structural sophistication via meter rather than via dynamic shift; tracks where compositional ambition extends beyond standard 4/4; verse-chorus contrasts that should feel different in body, not just in volume.
meter_shift_6_4_verse_to_4_4_chorus.trade_offs: Demands rhythm section comfortable in multiple meters + smooth transitions. Risk of feeling fussy if shifts not natural.
meter_shift_6_4_verse_to_4_4_chorus.documented_example: Fell On Black Days — verse in 6/4 shifts to 4/4 chorus, structural change marked rhythmically.

sus_voicings_verse_with_drop_tuning_peda.rule: In verse sections, use SUSPENDED chord voicings (sus2, sus4, modal voicings without 3rd) over a DROP-TUNED bass pedal. Suspended voicings keep harmony OPEN; drop-tuned pedal anchors low end. Verse harmony floats; chorus can resolve via 3rd.
sus_voicings_verse_with_drop_tuning_peda.applicability_text: Verses needing modal openness; songs where harmonic ambiguity is desired in verse but resolved in chorus; drop-tuned guitar contexts.
sus_voicings_verse_with_drop_tuning_peda.trade_offs: Demands tuning + voicing literacy. Risk of feeling vague if too many sections suspended without resolution.
sus_voicings_verse_with_drop_tuning_peda.documented_example: Fell On Black Days verses — suspended voicings over drop-tuned pedal create harmonic openness; chorus resolves.

clean_to_distorted_dynamic_shift_traditi.rule: Verse: clean (or lightly overdriven) guitar tone. Chorus: full distortion. The TONE SHIFT marks dynamic change, often paired with vocal projection lift. Traditional rock dynamic-arc mechanism — quiet-loud as guitar-tone-driven structure.
clean_to_distorted_dynamic_shift_traditi.applicability_text: Verse-chorus rock structures where dynamic shift is the song's mechanism; tracks following Pixies/Nirvana quiet-loud lineage; songs needing chorus 'lift' without volume mastering tricks.
clean_to_distorted_dynamic_shift_traditi.trade_offs: Genre-template mechanism. Risk of feeling formulaic if not paired with stronger compositional choices.
clean_to_distorted_dynamic_shift_traditi.documented_example: Fell On Black Days — clean verse to distorted chorus, Pixies/Nirvana-lineage quiet-loud mechanism.

obrien_moderate_compression_for_dynamic_.rule: When mixing dynamic-arc songs (verse quiet, chorus loud), use MODERATE compression rather than maximum — preserve the quiet-loud contrast that the composition relies on. Brendan O'Brien's documented Superunknown approach: shape impact at chorus without flattening verse intimacy.
obrien_moderate_compression_for_dynamic_.applicability_text: Mix decisions on dynamic-arc material; songs where over-compression would destroy the verse-chorus contrast; producer-mixer-split workflows.
obrien_moderate_compression_for_dynamic_.trade_offs: Less radio-impact than maximum compression. Demands mix engineer with restraint capacity.
obrien_moderate_compression_for_dynamic_.documented_example: Brendan O'Brien's mix of FOBD — moderate compression preserving the verse-chorus dynamic shift.

vocal_subselective_range_use.rule: Vocalist with documented wide range chooses to deploy ONLY a NARROW SUB-RANGE for the song. Restraint as compositional choice — the wide-range capacity is RESERVED rather than displayed. Listener experiences vocal restraint as artistic discipline, not lack of capacity.
vocal_subselective_range_use.applicability_text: Songs by vocalists known for wide range (Cornell, Mercury, Plant) that benefit from sub-selective restraint; tracks where vocal acrobatics would compete with arrangement; intimate sections within otherwise dynamic albums.
vocal_subselective_range_use.trade_offs: Demands documented wide-range vocalist who restrains. Risk of feeling underwhelming if listener expects acrobatics.
vocal_subselective_range_use.documented_example: FOBD — Cornell narrows his documented wide range to a sub-selective register for the song's reflective character.

five_four_groove_natural_via_groove_cons.rule: Compose in 5/4 meter but construct the groove so it FEELS NATURAL rather than mathematically odd. Group beats as 3+2 or 2+3 and build melodic + bass phrases that spell the groupings naturally. Listener feels the lilt without analyzing the meter.
five_four_groove_natural_via_groove_cons.applicability_text: Songs wanting odd-meter character without virtuosic complexity; tracks where rhythmic playfulness is the goal; bands with rhythm sections fluent in odd meters.
five_four_groove_natural_via_groove_cons.trade_offs: Demands rhythm section comfortable in 5/4 + groove-construction craft. Risk of feeling academic if grouping not naturalized.
five_four_groove_natural_via_groove_cons.documented_example: My Wave 5/4 throughout — Cameron's documented natural 5/4 groove (3+2 grouping) feels playful rather than odd.

drum_meter_pattern_groupings_natural_vs_.rule: When drumming odd meter (5/4, 7/4), choose the GROUPING that feels NATURAL to the song rather than the academically 'correct' one. 5/4 can be 3+2, 2+3, or even 1+1+1+1+1. Drummer's choice of grouping shapes the song's rhythmic identity.
drum_meter_pattern_groupings_natural_vs_.applicability_text: Odd-meter compositions where drummer has interpretive freedom; tracks where groove feel matters more than theoretical clarity; bands with drummers who internalize odd meters bodily.
drum_meter_pattern_groupings_natural_vs_.trade_offs: Demands drummer with internalized odd-meter capacity. Risk of feeling stiff if grouping forced.
drum_meter_pattern_groupings_natural_vs_.documented_example: My Wave — Cameron groups 5/4 as 3+2 naturally, drum pattern spells the grouping rather than counting it.

same_team_different_mix_per_song.rule: Within an album session, the SAME engineer-producer team can apply DIFFERENT MIX PHILOSOPHIES per song based on each song's compositional intent. No single mix template imposed across the album. Producer's craft is interpretive, not template-driven.
same_team_different_mix_per_song.applicability_text: Albums with diverse compositional intents per track; producer-band partnerships where producer has stylistic range; sessions where each song deserves its own mix decision.
same_team_different_mix_per_song.trade_offs: Demands producer with stylistic range + flexibility. Risk of album feeling inconsistent if mix variation too wide.
same_team_different_mix_per_song.documented_example: Beinhorn + O'Brien on Superunknown — same team, different mix philosophy per song depending on compositional intent.

bassist_dual_role_within_album.rule: Bassist plays DIFFERENT ROLES on different album tracks — supporting bass on some songs, melodically-leading bass on others, even compositional driver on co-written tracks. Bassist's range expands the album's compositional palette. Not just rhythm-section anchor.
bassist_dual_role_within_album.applicability_text: Albums where bassist has compositional contributions; tracks where bass is foreground (vs. supporting) for specific songs; bands willing to feature bass beyond rhythm-section role.
bassist_dual_role_within_album.trade_offs: Demands bassist with multi-role capacity. Risk of incoherent bass identity if roles too varied.
bassist_dual_role_within_album.documented_example: Ben Shepherd on Superunknown — bass plays supporting role on My Wave but compositional driver on Head Down.

tracking_priority_per_song_compositional.rule: At tracking stage, the PRIORITY ELEMENT changes per song based on compositional intent. One song prioritizes guitar wall; another prioritizes drum feel; another prioritizes vocal restraint. Engineer-producer adapts tracking priorities per song rather than imposing template.
tracking_priority_per_song_compositional.applicability_text: Albums with diverse compositional intents; producer-band relationships where producer can interpret per-song needs; sessions willing to spend extra time on song-specific tracking decisions.
tracking_priority_per_song_compositional.trade_offs: Adds session time + complexity. Demands producer with interpretive flexibility.
tracking_priority_per_song_compositional.documented_example: Beinhorn's Superunknown tracking — different priority per song (My Wave drum-feel vs. JCP guitar-wall vs. Black Hole Sun vocal).

extended_atmospheric_form_6_minutes_plus.rule: Build a 6+ minute rock track around an EXTENDED MIDDLE SECTION (3+ minutes of instrumental exploration). Verses + choruses become FRAMING for the central instrumental statement. Vocal absent for substantial duration trusts the band to carry listener attention via texture + harmonic motion.
extended_atmospheric_form_6_minutes_plus.applicability_text: Long-form rock/post-rock/psychedelic compositions where the listener should be carried through extended instrumental exploration. Use when the SONG'S IDENTITY is the bridge, not the verses/choruses.
extended_atmospheric_form_6_minutes_plus.trade_offs: Requires excellent musicians + atmospheric instrumental writing. Won't work in radio-format contexts.
extended_atmospheric_form_6_minutes_plus.documented_example: Overfloater (Soundgarden, Down on the Upside 1996, 6:42) — extended instrumental bridge is the song's identity.

instrumental_bridge_as_song_centerpiece_.rule: Make the BRIDGE the song's structural center (longest section, peak moment) rather than treating it as a transition. Bridge length should EXCEED chorus or verse length combined. Bridge harmonic motion should DIVERGE from verse/chorus pattern (modal, suspended, exploratory).
instrumental_bridge_as_song_centerpiece_.applicability_text: Compositions where the bridge is the song's identity; tracks where instrumental exploration deserves more compositional space than vocal sections.
instrumental_bridge_as_song_centerpiece_.trade_offs: Requires bridge to STAND on its own as compelling music — can't just be 'the part between sections'.
instrumental_bridge_as_song_centerpiece_.documented_example: Overfloater extended bridge — multiple minutes long, harmonically distinct from verse/chorus.

jam_band_influence_in_rock_format.rule: Borrow extended-form rock traditions (Pink Floyd, Led Zeppelin's longer tracks, Phish-adjacent jam sensibility) into a hard-rock or alt-rock context. Allow space for instrumental exploration without rushing back to vocal sections. The BAND's collective improvisation/arrangement carries the listener.
jam_band_influence_in_rock_format.applicability_text: Hard-rock/alt-rock compositions seeking extended-form ambition via jam-band tradition; bands with collective improvisation capacity; tracks willing to embrace cross-genre influence.
jam_band_influence_in_rock_format.trade_offs: Genre-crossing; risk alienating listeners expecting tight rock economy.
jam_band_influence_in_rock_format.documented_example: Overfloater bridge — jam-band-influenced extended texture in a hard-rock catalog.

vocal_absent_extended_section_trust_the_.rule: Have NO VOCAL for an extended section (2+ minutes) in a vocal-driven song. The instrumental texture must be compelling enough to hold attention without lyrical anchor. Used as DRAMATIC choice — the absence of voice becomes a structural statement.
vocal_absent_extended_section_trust_the_.applicability_text: Vocal-driven songs willing to sustain instrumental sections; tracks where listener should be carried by band texture; compositions wanting structural drama via vocal absence.
vocal_absent_extended_section_trust_the_.trade_offs: Listeners conditioned to expect vocal hooks may disengage. Requires confident instrumental writing.
vocal_absent_extended_section_trust_the_.documented_example: Overfloater multi-minute vocal-absent bridge — listener trusts band to carry them through.

kasper_band_driven_unified_engineering_m.rule: Use UNIFIED engineering + mixing roles (one person tracks AND mixes) to preserve recording-stage decisions through to final master. Avoid the producer-driven external-mixer split when the band's tracking aesthetic IS the desired final aesthetic. Result: more authentic, less sonically-shaped final product.
kasper_band_driven_unified_engineering_m.applicability_text: Late-career bands who know their sound; capture-focused projects; projects where authenticity matters more than commercial polish.
kasper_band_driven_unified_engineering_m.trade_offs: Less external sonic shaping; less radio-optimized; more authenticity-leaning. Requires excellent tracking decisions upfront.
kasper_band_driven_unified_engineering_m.documented_example: Adam Kasper engineered + co-produced + mixed Down on the Upside (1996) — band-driven, engineer-mixer unified role.

atmospheric_continuity_over_dynamic_cont.rule: Build a song where SUSTAINED ATMOSPHERIC CONTINUITY is the primary aesthetic, NOT quiet/loud dynamic contrast. Sections shift via harmonic + textural variation rather than via volume + intensity changes. Reverb + sustain + spacious mix support this.
atmospheric_continuity_over_dynamic_cont.applicability_text: Atmospheric tracks where dynamic shifts would feel cheap; songs prioritizing texture/harmony over architecture; bands with mix engineer who can sustain atmospheric depth.
atmospheric_continuity_over_dynamic_cont.trade_offs: Less obvious 'rock dynamics' for listeners conditioned to verse/chorus contrast. Requires harmonic + textural interest to sustain.
atmospheric_continuity_over_dynamic_cont.documented_example: Overfloater — atmospheric continuity across 6:42, no quiet/loud dynamic contrast as structural device.

seven_four_asymmetric_meter_as_structura.rule: Use 7/4 (or other long asymmetric meters: 11/8, 13/8) as STRUCTURAL DEVICE rather than as decorative complexity. The long asymmetric cycle should feel HYPNOTIC + STATELY — listeners inhabit the cycle. 7-beat groupings can subdivide as 4+3, 3+4, 3+2+2, or 2+3+2 depending on rhythmic emphasis.
seven_four_asymmetric_meter_as_structura.applicability_text: Atmospheric rock/post-rock/drone-doom-adjacent compositions where asymmetric meter + drone harmony serve meditative or hypnotic intent.
seven_four_asymmetric_meter_as_structura.trade_offs: Requires excellent drummer + bassist to make asymmetric meter FEEL natural rather than counted-stiffly. Listeners conditioned to 4/4 may need time to lock in.
seven_four_asymmetric_meter_as_structura.documented_example: Head Down (Soundgarden, Superunknown 1994, 7/4 throughout) — Cameron's 7/4 mastery makes it feel natural rather than arithmetic.

drone_foundation_as_compositional_anchor.rule: Build a song where SUSTAINED LOW-FREQUENCY DRONE is the harmonic gravity well. All other harmonic motion happens ABOVE the drone (suspended voicings, modal inflections) rather than via traditional chord progression. Drone should be present 80%+ of song duration.
drone_foundation_as_compositional_anchor.applicability_text: Atmospheric rock or drone-doom-adjacent compositions; tracks where harmonic stasis + textural exploration serve compositional intent.
drone_foundation_as_compositional_anchor.trade_offs: Requires excellent bass tone + sustain capability. May feel monotonous to listeners expecting harmonic motion.
drone_foundation_as_compositional_anchor.documented_example: Head Down — Shepherd's sustained low B drone foundation across 6:08.

modal_voicings_above_drone_replace_chord.rule: Instead of standard chord progression (I-IV-V-I or i-VI-VII-i), use SUSTAINED MODAL VOICINGS above a drone foundation. Suspensions (sus2, sus4), modal inflections (Aeolian, Phrygian), and quartal/quintal voicings preserve harmonic openness while creating subtle motion.
modal_voicings_above_drone_replace_chord.applicability_text: Compositions with drone foundation; tracks where harmonic openness is desired over progression-driven motion; modal-vocabulary contexts.
modal_voicings_above_drone_replace_chord.trade_offs: Less obvious 'songwriting' for listeners conditioned to hook-driven progressions. Requires harmonic ear to write convincingly.
modal_voicings_above_drone_replace_chord.documented_example: Head Down — modal voicings above drone foundation, harmonic motion via voicing shifts not chord changes.

bassist_driven_compositional_identity.rule: Allow the BASSIST to drive primary compositional identity (riff, structure, harmonic foundation) rather than always centering guitar riffs. Documented as Shepherd's role on Head Down — the bass drone IS the compositional engine.
bassist_driven_compositional_identity.applicability_text: Bands willing to feature bassist as compositional driver; songs where bass-line is the song's primary identity; tracks where bassist co-writes or solo-writes.
bassist_driven_compositional_identity.trade_offs: Requires bassist with compositional vision; band must trust bassist's structural ideas.
bassist_driven_compositional_identity.documented_example: Head Down — Ben Shepherd co-wrote, bass drone foundation is compositional anchor.

long_asymmetric_cycle_for_meditation_not.rule: When using asymmetric meter (7/4, 5/4, 11/8), let the cycle feel LONG + MEDITATIVE rather than tight + complex. Slow tempos (80-95 BPM) + sustained instrumentation help listeners INHABIT the cycle rather than count it. Asymmetric meter as MEDITATIVE STRUCTURAL DEVICE not as virtuosic flex.
long_asymmetric_cycle_for_meditation_not.applicability_text: Atmospheric tracks using odd meter; songs where the meter should feel meditative; bands willing to slow asymmetric meter into hypnotic cycle territory.
long_asymmetric_cycle_for_meditation_not.trade_offs: Requires aesthetic restraint — temptation to show off odd-meter playing must be resisted.
long_asymmetric_cycle_for_meditation_not.documented_example: Head Down 7/4 at ~88 BPM — meditative cycle, not virtuosic complexity.

low_frequency_clarity_priority_in_drone_.rule: When mixing drone-foundation arrangements, give the drone bass + low-tuned instruments DEDICATED low-frequency space. Coordinate kick (~60Hz) + bass drone (~80-120Hz) via complementary frequency placement. Avoid over-compressing the drone — preserve its natural envelope. Light dedicated reverb on drone-frequency layers can enhance spatial depth.
low_frequency_clarity_priority_in_drone_.applicability_text: Drone-based compositions in mix stage; tracks where low-end clarity is critical to the song's compositional foundation.
low_frequency_clarity_priority_in_drone_.trade_offs: Requires careful frequency carving + restraint with compression.
low_frequency_clarity_priority_in_drone_.documented_example: Head Down O'Brien mix — bass drone clearly audible without becoming muddy.

wall_of_layered_guitars_frequency_spread.rule: Layer multiple guitar tracks (5-15+) panned across stereo field + EQ-staggered to occupy DIFFERENT FREQUENCY SLOTS (low-mid, mid-range, upper-mid, high). Result: wall reads as ONE BIG GUITAR rather than as cluttered multiple guitars. Each layer can have different distortion character (different amps/pedals) for textural variation.
wall_of_layered_guitars_frequency_spread.electronic_translation: Build a synth-stack of 5-15 SUSTAINED PADS / processed-sample layers, each with different source (analog VA mono, granular pad from Output Substance, FM bell from Operator, bowed-string from Spitfire LABS, vocoded sustain, noise-bed from Reaktor) and EQ-staggered to occupy distinct frequency slots: layer 1 sub (40-80Hz), layer 2 low-mid (150-300Hz), layer 3 mid (500-1k), layer 4 upper-mid (2-4k), layer 5+ high (6-12k). Pan hard L / soft L / center / soft R / hard R / wide-stereo. Each layer can have different saturation character (Decapitator on one, FabFilter Saturn on another, hardware tape on a third for analog warmth). Wall reads as ONE BIG TEXTURE — same compositional principle as Quiet/Siamese Dream, but in synth-stack instead of guitar-stack. Apply for: peak chorus density in techno/trance peaks, ambient texture climax, dark-electronic drop, synthwave wall-of-sound chorus. Avoid in minimum-element-count or filter-house contexts (incompatible aesthetics).
wall_of_layered_guitars_frequency_spread.applicability_text: Heavy alt-rock or modern-rock compositions where dense guitar layering serves a tight commercial economy. Use when the SONG'S IDENTITY is the riff + the wall, not dynamic arc.
wall_of_layered_guitars_frequency_spread.trade_offs: Time-intensive to track + mix correctly. Risk of mud if EQ-staggering not careful. Requires patient producer/mixer (Vig + Wallace).
wall_of_layered_guitars_frequency_spread.documented_example: Quiet (Smashing Pumpkins, Siamese Dream 1993) + entire Siamese Dream album — Corgan's documented multi-layered guitar philosophy.

riff_as_both_hook_and_structural_backbon.rule: Write a guitar riff that is SIMULTANEOUSLY the song's hook AND its structural backbone (continues through verses + reappears in choruses). Guitar riff carries melodic + harmonic + rhythmic identity. Vocal hook becomes secondary to riff hook.
riff_as_both_hook_and_structural_backbon.applicability_text: Heavy rock songs where the riff IS the song; tracks where guitar identity supersedes vocal identity; commercial-rock contexts wanting riff-driven memorability.
riff_as_both_hook_and_structural_backbon.trade_offs: Requires strong riff-writing capacity. Listeners conditioned to vocal-hook pop may need adjustment.
riff_as_both_hook_and_structural_backbon.documented_example: Quiet opening riff — both hook and structure.

sustained_intensity_no_dynamic_arc_econo.rule: Build a heavy rock song that stays HEAVY throughout — no quiet/loud dynamic contrast, no clean intro, no atmospheric bridge. Sustained intensity for full runtime (3-4 minutes for tight commercial economy). Choruses can EXPAND density but should not REDUCE intensity.
sustained_intensity_no_dynamic_arc_econo.applicability_text: Commercial-rock contexts wanting sustained intensity; tracks following Quiet/JCP-lineage compositional approach; songs where heaviness IS the identity.
sustained_intensity_no_dynamic_arc_econo.trade_offs: Less obvious 'song peak' for listeners conditioned to verse-chorus dynamic build. Requires strong sustained material to prevent monotony.
sustained_intensity_no_dynamic_arc_econo.documented_example: Quiet 3:42 sustained heavy intensity — no dynamic arc within song, but album includes dynamic-arc tracks for variety.

vig_meticulous_layering_recording_philos.rule: When recording multi-layered guitar arrangements, capture EACH LAYER cleanly (different mic placement, amp, pedal) so that subsequent mix layering reads clearly. Use parallel compression on drum bus for weight + transient detail. Engineer-producer role facilitates band's vision rather than imposing producer's template.
vig_meticulous_layering_recording_philos.applicability_text: Wall-of-sound recording sessions; bands with strong layering vision needing meticulous capture; producer role configured as facilitator.
vig_meticulous_layering_recording_philos.documented_example: Butch Vig's documented Siamese Dream approach — meticulous layering capture, parallel-compressed drum bus.

wallace_stereo_width_maximization_mix.rule: When mixing wall-of-sound arrangements, maximize STEREO WIDTH via aggressive L/R panning of guitar layers + frequency-spread EQ. Vocal forward in mix to read clearly above density. Drums punchy + present (carry tracking-stage parallel compression through). Reverb moderate — layering itself provides spatial depth.
wallace_stereo_width_maximization_mix.applicability_text: Wall-of-sound mix sessions; tracks where stereo impact is part of the song's compositional intent; commercial-rock contexts.
wallace_stereo_width_maximization_mix.trade_offs: Mono-compatibility risk if width pushed too far; requires careful phase management.
wallace_stereo_width_maximization_mix.documented_example: Andy Wallace mix on Siamese Dream Quiet — stereo width maximization, frequency-spread layer management.

extended_dynamic_arc_six_plus_minutes_pa.rule: Build a 6+ minute song around an EXTENDED DYNAMIC ARC: sparse clean intro (1-2 min) → restrained verses (1-2 min) → bridge buildup (1-1.5 min) → MASSIVE EXPLOSION CHORUS (1-2 min) → resolution back to clean (30s-1 min). The patience IS the engine — listeners must INVEST attention for the explosion to land.
extended_dynamic_arc_six_plus_minutes_pa.electronic_translation: Compose 6-9 min track with documented density arc, planned as Live arrangement-view sections: INTRO (1-2 min, single sustained pad + sub-bass + atmospheric ticks), VERSE BUILD (1-2 min, add filtered hi-hats + restrained kick on 1+3 + processed-vocal whispers), BRIDGE BUILDUP (1-1.5 min, full kick pattern + risers + automated low-end pumping + filter-cutoff climbing toward peak), EXPLOSION (1-2 min, peak density: full drums + bass-line + 5+ synth-pad layers + lead synth + saturated vocal-as-instrument), RESOLUTION (30s-1 min, strip layers, return to single pad + reverb tail). The 'explosion' is electronic-density-peak, not rock-distortion-explosion. Apply for: techno arrangement-view tradition (peak-time DJ tracks), IDM long-form, ambient-techno, downtempo emotional arcs, neo-classical-electronic ambient-builds. The patience demand is REAL in techno (Berghain crowd will follow 7 min) but won't work in radio-edit contexts.
extended_dynamic_arc_six_plus_minutes_pa.applicability_text: Long-form rock/post-rock compositions where slow patient build to massive explosion is the song's identity. Use when the LISTENER'S INVESTMENT in the build is the foundation for the explosion's emotional impact.
extended_dynamic_arc_six_plus_minutes_pa.trade_offs: Demands listener patience; risks attentional loss in compressed listening contexts. Requires excellent material in restrained sections to keep listener engaged.
extended_dynamic_arc_six_plus_minutes_pa.documented_example: Soma (Smashing Pumpkins, Siamese Dream 1993, 6:39) — extended dynamic arc as composition's emotional engine.

density_arc_arrangement_sparse_to_wall.rule: Arrange so density GROWS across the song — start with minimum elements (clean guitar + sparse drums), add layers progressively, peak at maximum density during explosion, resolve to minimum at outro. Each section ADDS rather than reshuffles.
density_arc_arrangement_sparse_to_wall.applicability_text: Dynamic-arc songs where arrangement growth is the build mechanism; tracks needing visible 'fillout' from sparse to wall.
density_arc_arrangement_sparse_to_wall.trade_offs: Requires patience in early sections; layering must be deployed at right moments.
density_arc_arrangement_sparse_to_wall.documented_example: Soma arrangement — clean intro to maximum-density explosion to clean outro.

selective_distortion_deployment_across_a.rule: DEPLOY distortion selectively across the song's dynamic arc — clean in intro/early verses, partial distortion in first chorus (preview), FULL distortion in explosion. Distortion as COMPOSITIONAL CHOICE, not as constant texture.
selective_distortion_deployment_across_a.applicability_text: Dynamic-arc rock songs; tracks where guitar tone shift marks structural progression; multi-track guitar capacity.
selective_distortion_deployment_across_a.trade_offs: Requires multiple guitar tracks tracked separately; requires mix discipline to deploy correctly.
selective_distortion_deployment_across_a.documented_example: Soma guitars — clean to partial-distortion to FULL wall-of-distortion in explosion.

explosion_chorus_as_cathartic_release_no.rule: Treat the EXPLOSION CHORUS as the song's emotional + cathartic CULMINATION, not just as a loud section. Build prepared listener via patient earlier sections. Explosion should feel INEVITABLE + EARNED. Maximum density (guitar wall + full drums + vocal projection) deployed only here.
explosion_chorus_as_cathartic_release_no.applicability_text: Dynamic-arc songs where the chorus carries cathartic weight; tracks where the loudest moment is also the emotional peak; rock-anthem contexts.
explosion_chorus_as_cathartic_release_no.trade_offs: If build doesn't earn it, explosion sounds gratuitous. Risk of anti-climax if execution falters.
explosion_chorus_as_cathartic_release_no.documented_example: Soma's explosion chorus after 4+ minutes of build — feels cathartic, not gratuitous.

dynamic_arc_mix_preservation_via_automat.rule: When mixing dynamic-arc compositions, use mix automation to RIDE LEVELS across the arc — drum bus, guitar layers, vocal, reverb sends all ride up + down through the arc. Avoid full-mix compression that would flatten the arc. Restrained sections stay RESTRAINED in the master.
dynamic_arc_mix_preservation_via_automat.applicability_text: Mix sessions for dynamic-arc material; tracks where verse-chorus contrast must be preserved; mix engineers willing to time-invest in automation.
dynamic_arc_mix_preservation_via_automat.trade_offs: Time-intensive mixing; requires mixer who understands compositional intent. May read 'quieter' than over-compressed contemporary masters.
dynamic_arc_mix_preservation_via_automat.documented_example: Andy Wallace's Soma mix — dynamic arc preservation via extensive automation.

patience_demand_as_compositional_choice.rule: Make a long song where the LISTENER'S PATIENCE is itself part of the compositional contract. Trust the audience to invest attention if the material in early sections is strong enough to hold them. PATIENCE-DEMANDING composition is a structural choice that creates a different listener-relationship than economy-driven compression.
patience_demand_as_compositional_choice.applicability_text: Album tracks (not radio singles); compositions targeting attentive-listener audiences; tracks where the build is the song.
patience_demand_as_compositional_choice.trade_offs: Won't work in radio/playlist contexts. Demands artistic confidence.
patience_demand_as_compositional_choice.documented_example: Soma 6:39 trusting listener to wait through patient build for explosion.

fast_tempo_148_156_bpm_aggressive_heavy_.rule: Push tempo into 145-160 BPM territory for a heavy rock track — faster than mainstream alt-rock standard (90-130 BPM) but retaining wall-of-density sonics. Fast tempo + sustained intensity + chromatic riff motion = punk-influenced heavy rock economy.
fast_tempo_148_156_bpm_aggressive_heavy_.applicability_text: Heavy rock tracks wanting punk-leaning fast tempo without abandoning wall-of-density; bands with rhythm sections capable at fast tempo; commercial-rock contexts wanting tempo-driven aggression.

source.album: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

fast_tempo_148_156_bpm_aggressive_heavy_.trade_offs: Demands tight musicianship at fast tempo; risk of muddy mix if layering not well-managed.
fast_tempo_148_156_bpm_aggressive_heavy_.documented_example: Bodies (Smashing Pumpkins, Mellon Collie 1995, 148-156 BPM) — fast aggressive heavy rock with retained Pumpkins layering.

fast_tempo_layered_guitar_density_manage.rule: When layering multiple guitars at fast tempo (140+ BPM), prioritize RHYTHMIC ARTICULATION over maximum stereo width. EQ-staggered layers must align rhythmically — picking precision required across all takes. Mix tighter stereo than at slower tempos to preserve transient detail.
fast_tempo_layered_guitar_density_manage.applicability_text: Fast-tempo wall-of-sound recordings; bands with picking precision; mix sessions where rhythmic clarity matters more than stereo impressiveness.

source.album: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

fast_tempo_layered_guitar_density_manage.trade_offs: Less stereo-width impact than slower-tempo wall-of-sound; requires picking precision in tracking.
fast_tempo_layered_guitar_density_manage.documented_example: Bodies guitar layering at fast tempo — rhythmic articulation prioritized via Flood+Moulder mix.

deliberate_sonic_evolution_between_album.rule: DELIBERATELY change producer/engineer team between albums to evolve sonic identity. Same band, different production team = different sonic palette. Avoids stagnation; demonstrates band's willingness to push their sound. Strategic for bands with multi-album arc.
deliberate_sonic_evolution_between_album.applicability_text: Established bands risking stagnation; multi-album catalog planning; bands willing to challenge audience expectations via production evolution.

source.album: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

deliberate_sonic_evolution_between_album.trade_offs: Risk of fan alienation if too-different. Requires confidence to change established formula.
deliberate_sonic_evolution_between_album.documented_example: Smashing Pumpkins: Vig+Wallace (Siamese Dream 1993) → Flood+Moulder+Corgan (Mellon Collie 1995). Deliberate evolution.

three_way_producer_collective_band_membe.rule: Use a THREE-WAY producer credit including a band member as full producer alongside two external producers. Distributes decision-making, gives band-member-producer creative authority while leveraging external sonic perspectives. Avoids both pure-band-driven (no external perspective) and pure-producer-imposed (no band agency).
three_way_producer_collective_band_membe.applicability_text: Established bands wanting compositional control + external sonic input; producer-band relationships willing to distribute authority across three creators; multi-album projects.

source.album: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

three_way_producer_collective_band_membe.trade_offs: Can result in creative disagreements; requires strong communication. Three-way decision-making slower than two-way.
three_way_producer_collective_band_membe.documented_example: Flood + Alan Moulder + Billy Corgan three-way producer credit on Mellon Collie.

moulder_mix_rhythmic_aggression_priority.rule: When mixing fast-tempo aggressive heavy rock, prioritize RHYTHMIC ARTICULATION over maximum stereo width. Tighter stereo + drum-bus weight + transient detail preservation = fast-tempo clarity. Less reverb than slower atmospheric mixes (reverb smears rhythm).
moulder_mix_rhythmic_aggression_priority.applicability_text: Mix sessions for fast-tempo aggressive rock; tracks where rhythmic clarity is the goal; Moulder-style or heavy-rock-specialist mix engineers.

source.album: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

moulder_mix_rhythmic_aggression_priority.trade_offs: Less spectacular stereo impact than Wallace-style maximized width.
moulder_mix_rhythmic_aggression_priority.documented_example: Alan Moulder's Bodies mix — rhythmic aggression priority.

industrial_leaning_rhythmic_emphasis_wit.rule: Build a heavy rock track that ABSORBS industrial influence (rhythmic precision, drum-machine-evocative live drumming, possible processed textures) while retaining ROCK IDENTITY (live drums, riff-based composition, vocal-as-vocal not as sample). The genre-blend is the song's character.
industrial_leaning_rhythmic_emphasis_wit.applicability_text: Alt-rock or heavy-rock contexts wanting industrial-adjacent rhythmic emphasis without abandoning rock identity; bands willing to embrace cross-genre influence.

source.album: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

industrial_leaning_rhythmic_emphasis_wit.trade_offs: Risk of feeling neither-fish-nor-fowl if balance off. Requires production team with cross-genre experience.
industrial_leaning_rhythmic_emphasis_wit.documented_example: Zero (Smashing Pumpkins, Mellon Collie 1995, 2:40) — alt-rock band INFLUENCED BY industrial via Moulder's NIN-translation.

compressed_economy_under_three_minutes_i.rule: Within an album that includes long-form tracks (5+ minutes), include a SHORT (under 3 min) HIGH-INTENSITY track that demonstrates compositional economy. The short track reads as RELENTLESSLY EFFICIENT against the album's longer pieces. Compositional 'movement' hyper-dense.
compressed_economy_under_three_minutes_i.applicability_text: Multi-track albums with diverse runtime ambitions; tracks meant as palette-cleansers; songs proving compositional discipline within ambitious albums.

source.album: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

compressed_economy_under_three_minutes_i.trade_offs: Demands strong material since duration doesn't allow development. Risk of feeling like sketch rather than song.
compressed_economy_under_three_minutes_i.documented_example: Zero 2:40 within Mellon Collie (28-track double album with multiple 5-9 min epic tracks) — compressed economy track among long-form pieces.

rhythmic_emphasis_over_harmonic_movement.rule: Foreground RHYTHM over harmonic motion — riff's rhythmic identity carries the song, harmonic stasis acceptable as platform for rhythmic emphasis. Borrowed from industrial influence (NIN, Ministry, Front 242) where rhythmic precision is primary compositional axis.
rhythmic_emphasis_over_harmonic_movement.applicability_text: Industrial-leaning rock or electronic-rock contexts; tracks where rhythm is primary compositional axis; bands willing to subordinate harmonic movement to rhythmic identity.

source.album: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

rhythmic_emphasis_over_harmonic_movement.trade_offs: Requires strong rhythmic-riff writing capacity; listeners conditioned to harmonic motion may need adjustment.
rhythmic_emphasis_over_harmonic_movement.documented_example: Zero — chromatic riff with rhythmic identity primary, harmonic movement minimal.

tighter_layering_for_rhythmic_clarity_ge.rule: When blending genres (rock + industrial), REDUCE layering count compared to pure-rock-wall approach. Tighter layering serves rhythmic clarity (industrial aesthetic) while retaining rock textural variety. Each layer EQ-staggered + panned for stereo width but not maxed.
tighter_layering_for_rhythmic_clarity_ge.applicability_text: Genre-blend recordings where rhythmic clarity matters more than wall-of-density; bands transitioning from pure rock to industrial-influenced; mix sessions for hybrid material.

source.album: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

tighter_layering_for_rhythmic_clarity_ge.trade_offs: Less stereo-width impact than wall-of-sound; requires strong individual layers since fewer to choose from.
tighter_layering_for_rhythmic_clarity_ge.documented_example: Zero guitar layering tighter than Quiet's Siamese Dream wall — appropriate for industrial-leaning intent.

moulder_industrial_translation_alt_rock_.rule: Hire mix engineer with cross-genre experience (e.g., industrial-rock translator) when band wants to absorb cross-genre influence. Engineer's prior experience translates the foreign influence into band's own genre context. Mix decisions reflect cross-genre fluency.
moulder_industrial_translation_alt_rock_.applicability_text: Bands seeking cross-genre influence absorption; mix engineer hiring decisions for genre-fusion projects; established engineer-as-translator workflows.

source.album: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

moulder_industrial_translation_alt_rock_.trade_offs: Engineer must have genuine cross-genre fluency, not just nominal experience. Risk of forced-feeling translation if engineer-band fit weak.
moulder_industrial_translation_alt_rock_.documented_example: Alan Moulder (NIN's Downward Spiral mix engineer) mixing Smashing Pumpkins' Mellon Collie — industrial-into-alt-rock translation.

industrial_drum_processing_on_live_drums.rule: Process LIVE drums to evoke industrial-drum-machine character without replacing them with samples. Parallel saturation + gated reverbs + tight compression create industrial-adjacent feel while preserving live drumming nuance. The drums sound INDUSTRIAL-EVOCATIVE but remain LIVE.
industrial_drum_processing_on_live_drums.applicability_text: Hybrid genre projects with live drums + industrial aesthetic; mix sessions for genre-blend material; bands willing to process live drums heavily.

source.album: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

industrial_drum_processing_on_live_drums.trade_offs: Over-processing kills the live feel; requires careful balance.
industrial_drum_processing_on_live_drums.documented_example: Zero drum processing — Chamberlin's live drums treated for industrial feel via Moulder's NIN-influenced techniques.

asymmetric_meter_at_extreme_tempo.rule: Use 7/8 + 8/8 alternation (or other asymmetric groupings) at TEMPOS ABOVE 200 BPM. The asymmetric grouping creates constant rhythmic tension; the extreme tempo creates relentless forward pressure. Together they create a UNIQUE FEEL — neither pure groove nor pure complexity, but SUSTAINED OFF-BALANCE.
asymmetric_meter_at_extreme_tempo.electronic_translation: Program drums in DAW step-sequencer at the asymmetric grouping (e.g., 5 bars of 7/8 followed by 1 bar of 8/8) — Live's signature/time-grid lets you define non-4/4 meters. Use AGGRESSIVE PROGRAMMED DRUMS (TR-909 + 808 + sample-triggered metallic hits) at 200-265 BPM, not live drums. Bass: distorted Operator/Massive bass synth on tonic, locked to kick. Texture: heavy saturation + bit-crush on percussion bus, sidechain everything to kick. The asymmetric cycle becomes the song's identity — listeners feel 'wrong-but-driving'. Apply for industrial-techno breakdowns, dark-electronic peak sections, EBM intensity statements, IDM rhythmic showpieces. Avoid in deep-house or ambient (incompatible with their meditative pulse).
asymmetric_meter_at_extreme_tempo.applicability_text: Industrial-rock or extreme-metal contexts wanting rhythmic extremity; programmed-drum capacity required for sustained extreme tempo.
asymmetric_meter_at_extreme_tempo.trade_offs: Live performance challenging — programming required for sustained tempo. Listeners conditioned to 4/4 may need adjustment.
asymmetric_meter_at_extreme_tempo.documented_example: March of the Pigs (NIN, Downward Spiral 1994, 7/8+8/8 at ~265 BPM).

hard_soft_hard_contrast_within_song.rule: Build a song where the AGGRESSIVE-MELODIC-AGGRESSIVE contrast happens WITHIN section transitions, not just between separate songs. Verse aggressive → chorus suddenly melodic + clean → verse returns to aggression. Brief (4-8 measures) chorus melodic relief amid sustained aggression. Compositional weapon for emotional impact.
hard_soft_hard_contrast_within_song.applicability_text: Aggressive industrial or hard-rock tracks where brief melodic relief amplifies aggression; songs wanting compositional contrast within tight runtime.
hard_soft_hard_contrast_within_song.trade_offs: Risk of jarring transitions if execution off. Requires composing TWO opposed musical materials and integrating them.
hard_soft_hard_contrast_within_song.documented_example: March of the Pigs verse-aggression to brief melodic chorus to verse-aggression — Reznor signature compositional move.

programmed_drums_as_compositional_engine.rule: Treat programmed drums + sampled percussion as COMPOSITIONAL INSTRUMENTS — programming choices ARE composition. Use programming to enable tempos + meters live drumming cannot sustain. Layer multiple percussion elements (kick + snare foundation + industrial samples + textural percussion) as composed sonic field.
programmed_drums_as_compositional_engine.applicability_text: Industrial-rock or electronic-rock projects; tracks where programmed drums are creative center not technical fallback; compositions using extreme tempos/meters.
programmed_drums_as_compositional_engine.trade_offs: Demands programming skill; requires shift from drummer-band paradigm. Live performance requires careful planning.
programmed_drums_as_compositional_engine.documented_example: March of the Pigs Reznor-programmed drums at extreme tempo + asymmetric meter.

auteur_producer_single_creator_driven_pr.rule: Have ONE PERSON handle writing + most performance + production + much engineering — the auteur-producer model. Single creative vision unified across all production decisions. No band democracy, no producer-band collaboration. Suits artists with strong solo creative vision + technical capacity.
auteur_producer_single_creator_driven_pr.electronic_translation: ELECTRONIC MUSIC IS NATURALLY AUTEUR-FRIENDLY: solo bedroom-producer working in Live/Logic/FL Studio handles writing + sound design + performance (everything via MIDI/automation) + tracking (no live mics needed for synths) + mixing in-the-box. The auteur-producer is the DEFAULT mode for solo electronic projects (Aphex Twin, Burial, Four Tet, Jon Hopkins). Make a deliberate choice though: auteur model = total creative control + slow iteration / collab model = external ear + faster iteration. For dark-electronic / IDM / glitch / experimental projects, auteur model preserves idiosyncrasy. For commercial techno / deep house targeting club rotation, hire an external mixing engineer at the end (preserves auteur composition + benefits from external mix perspective on club-system translation). Tooling: build a standardized template (Live racks, custom drum kits, signature reverb chains) so the auteur-mode session is repeatable.
auteur_producer_single_creator_driven_pr.applicability_text: Solo artists with broad technical+creative capacity; projects where single creative vision is paramount; industrial/electronic-rock contexts.
auteur_producer_single_creator_driven_pr.trade_offs: Demands single creator's technical + creative competence at high level. Loses external perspective. Risk of insular creative work.
auteur_producer_single_creator_driven_pr.documented_example: Trent Reznor's Downward Spiral auteur-producer model (with Sean Beavan engineering).

scream_to_sing_vocal_contrast_as_composi.rule: Have vocalist execute SCREAM-TO-SING contrast within same song — verse screamed, chorus sung melodically, return to screamed. Vocal contrast as EMOTIONAL ENGINE. Different processing chains for screamed vs. sung sections (saturated vs. clean).
scream_to_sing_vocal_contrast_as_composi.applicability_text: Songs leveraging vocalist's full register; tracks where vocal contrast IS the song's emotional engine; production sessions willing to track multiple vocal processing chains.
scream_to_sing_vocal_contrast_as_composi.trade_offs: Requires vocalist with both capacities. Production must support both with different processing.
scream_to_sing_vocal_contrast_as_composi.documented_example: Reznor's March of the Pigs vocal scream-to-sing contrast.

industrial_textural_aggression_via_proce.rule: Use guitars as TEXTURAL AGGRESSION layers rather than as harmonic-melodic primary. Heavy distortion + saturation + possibly re-amping + sustained noise/feedback elements. Bass synth + programmed drums carry harmonic-rhythmic primary; guitars provide AGGRESSIVE COLOR.
industrial_textural_aggression_via_proce.applicability_text: Industrial-rock contexts where guitars serve textural rather than melodic function; productions willing to invert rock-band guitar-as-primary paradigm.
industrial_textural_aggression_via_proce.trade_offs: Reverses rock guitar paradigm; may feel wrong to rock guitarists. Demands strong synthesizer + sample programming to compensate.
industrial_textural_aggression_via_proce.documented_example: March of the Pigs guitars — textural aggression rather than harmonic primary.

auteur_recording_environment_house_studi.rule: Record in a NON-COMMERCIAL space with documented atmospheric/historical character. The space CONTRIBUTES to the album's mood + mythology. Examples: home studios, converted houses, historical buildings. Auteur producer can choose space matching artistic vision.
auteur_recording_environment_house_studi.applicability_text: Auteur-driven projects where recording space contributes to the album's mood; non-commercial production contexts; artists with documented site-specific mythology.
auteur_recording_environment_house_studi.trade_offs: Acoustic challenges of non-commercial spaces; requires technical skill to overcome. Mythology can overshadow music.
auteur_recording_environment_house_studi.documented_example: Le Pig Studio at 10050 Cielo Drive (Tate house) for Downward Spiral — documented dark mythology contribution.

minimum_viable_element_count_for_emotion.rule: Strip arrangement to FEWEST POSSIBLE ELEMENTS while maintaining emotional impact. Each element must EARN its presence. Synth-pad foundation + sparse percussion + vocal + bass = often sufficient. Density discipline = emotional clarity.
minimum_viable_element_count_for_emotion.applicability_text: Atmospheric ballad or post-rock compositions; tracks where emotional vulnerability is the song's identity; productions willing to subtract rather than add.
minimum_viable_element_count_for_emotion.trade_offs: Demands strong material since each element scrutinized. No 'hide in density' option. Risk of feeling thin if material weak.

atmospheric_sustain_as_compositional_fou.rule: Build composition on SUSTAINED SYNTH-PAD textures providing harmonic field. Pads layered (multiple pads EQ-staggered) for frequency-spread depth. Pad sustain replaces traditional rhythmic-harmonic-foundation role of guitar/piano in band format.
atmospheric_sustain_as_compositional_fou.applicability_text: Atmospheric or art-rock ballad compositions; productions using synth pads as structural foundation; tracks where sustain replaces rhythmic foundation.
atmospheric_sustain_as_compositional_fou.trade_offs: Requires synthesizer + sample expertise. Less viable in pure-band-format contexts.

auteur_framework_serving_opposite_emotio.rule: Use SAME auteur-producer framework to make OPPOSITE-POLE compositions on different albums or different songs within an album. Industrial extremity AND atmospheric ballad from same creator framework. Demonstrates artist's range; expands audience expectations.
auteur_framework_serving_opposite_emotio.applicability_text: Established auteur-producers seeking range demonstration; multi-album catalog planning; tracks that deliberately challenge audience expectations.
auteur_framework_serving_opposite_emotio.trade_offs: Risk of audience confusion if range too wide. Demands artistic confidence to challenge audience expectations.
auteur_framework_serving_opposite_emotio.documented_example: Reznor auteur framework: March of the Pigs (1994 industrial extremity) AND TDTWWA (1999 atmospheric ballad). Opposite poles, same auteur.

vulnerable_vocal_intimacy_clean_processi.rule: When recording emotionally vulnerable vocal, use CLEAN PROCESSING (no saturation/distortion), DEEP REVERB (plate + hall), NO PITCH CORRECTION (preserves raw emotional quality), MULTIPLE COMPLETE TAKES (best emotional moment selected, not comped per-syllable). Recording environment must be intimate + comfortable.
vulnerable_vocal_intimacy_clean_processi.applicability_text: Vulnerable vocal performances; ballad-format intimate tracks; productions valuing emotional rawness over technical perfection.
vulnerable_vocal_intimacy_clean_processi.trade_offs: Demands vocalist comfortable with vulnerability. Demands engineer/producer who can preserve intimacy.
vulnerable_vocal_intimacy_clean_processi.documented_example: Reznor's TDTWWA vocal — most vulnerable in NIN catalog, clean processing, full reverb, raw take selection.

gradual_subtle_build_not_dynamic_arc_exp.rule: Build emotional climax via VERY GRADUAL density addition rather than via dramatic dynamic-arc explosion. Subtle escalations across 4-5 minute song — additional synth layer, drum density slightly more active, vocal slightly more invested. Emotional release is FELT MORE THAN HEARD.
gradual_subtle_build_not_dynamic_arc_exp.applicability_text: Atmospheric ballad compositions; tracks where subtlety is the build mechanism; songs avoiding rock-anthem dynamic-arc template.
gradual_subtle_build_not_dynamic_arc_exp.trade_offs: Less obvious 'song peak' for listeners conditioned to dynamic-arc rock structure. Demands compositional confidence.
gradual_subtle_build_not_dynamic_arc_exp.documented_example: TDTWWA gradual subtle build vs. Soma's explosive dynamic-arc — two different scales of emotional payoff.

drum_machine_serving_atmospheric_emotion.rule: Use programmed drums + samples for ATMOSPHERIC composition, not just for aggressive industrial-tempo extremity. Sparse kick pulse + atmospheric percussion samples + processed treatments = drum-machine-serving-atmosphere. Demonstrates programming's emotional range.
drum_machine_serving_atmospheric_emotion.applicability_text: Atmospheric productions using programmed drums; tracks where drum machine serves restraint not aggression; bands willing to use drum machines for ballad-format material.
drum_machine_serving_atmospheric_emotion.trade_offs: Demands restraint with programming tools (temptation to 'fill the grid' must be resisted).
drum_machine_serving_atmospheric_emotion.documented_example: TDTWWA programmed drums — minimal sparse atmospheric vs. March of the Pigs maximum aggressive industrial.

moulder_atmospheric_mix_sustain_preserva.rule: When mixing atmospheric compositions, MAXIMIZE reverb on atmospheric elements (long tails, hall + plate blends), SPREAD synth-pad layers wide for harmonic field depth, place vocal centered + forward for emotional anchor, AVOID aggressive compression that would flatten atmospheric breathing.
moulder_atmospheric_mix_sustain_preserva.applicability_text: Atmospheric mix sessions; tracks where reverb tails are critical to compositional intent; productions valuing space over impact.
moulder_atmospheric_mix_sustain_preserva.trade_offs: Requires patience + trust in sustained elements. May read 'quieter' than aggressive contemporary masters.
moulder_atmospheric_mix_sustain_preserva.documented_example: Alan Moulder's TDTWWA mix — atmospheric sustain preservation, intimate vocal placement.

industrial_vocabulary_in_anthemic_rock_s.rule: Combine industrial PRODUCTION VOCABULARY (programmed drums, processed textures, distorted bass synth) with TRADITIONAL ROCK ANTHEM STRUCTURE (verse-chorus-bridge-final-chorus). The HYBRID is the song's identity — neither pure industrial nor pure rock, but their INTEGRATED FUSION.
industrial_vocabulary_in_anthemic_rock_s.applicability_text: Industrial-rock or electronic-rock-fusion compositions; tracks targeting anthemic emotional reach via traditional structure with industrial textures.
industrial_vocabulary_in_anthemic_rock_s.trade_offs: Risk of feeling neither-fish-nor-fowl if balance off. Demands strong material in both genre-vocabularies.
industrial_vocabulary_in_anthemic_rock_s.documented_example: We're In This Together (NIN, Fragile 1999, 7:16) — industrial vocabulary in anthemic rock structure.

extended_form_anthem_seven_plus_minutes.rule: Extend anthemic rock track beyond commercial-single economy (7+ minutes) to allow multiple chorus repetitions with INTENSIFYING EMOTIONAL WEIGHT across iterations. Final chorus repetitions feel cumulatively more powerful than first chorus due to extended setup.
extended_form_anthem_seven_plus_minutes.applicability_text: Anthemic rock or industrial-rock-anthem compositions willing to extend; tracks where emotional cumulative weight is the build mechanism.
extended_form_anthem_seven_plus_minutes.trade_offs: Demands listener patience; risks losing attention if material not strong enough to sustain. Won't work in radio/playlist contexts.
extended_form_anthem_seven_plus_minutes.documented_example: We're In This Together 7:16 with multiple chorus iterations intensifying emotional weight.

auteur_framework_supporting_opposite_int.rule: Use SAME auteur-producer framework to make opposite-intent compositions WITHIN SAME ALBUM SESSION. Atmospheric vulnerability AND anthemic emotional power AND industrial extremity all from same auteur. Album becomes EXPLORATION OF AUTEUR'S RANGE rather than singular-statement.
auteur_framework_supporting_opposite_int.applicability_text: Multi-track albums by auteur-producers wanting range demonstration within single album; sessions willing to support diverse compositional intents.
auteur_framework_supporting_opposite_int.trade_offs: Requires extended album form to allow range demonstration. Risk of audience confusion if range too wide.
auteur_framework_supporting_opposite_int.documented_example: The Fragile (NIN 1999) — same Reznor+Moulder framework supports TDTWWA atmospheric, We're In This Together anthemic, plus aggressive tracks like The Wretched within same album.

multiple_chorus_iterations_with_intensif.rule: Build the song so MULTIPLE chorus iterations occur (3-5 chorus repetitions across extended runtime), with each iteration progressively MORE INTENSE — additional vocal layering, additional guitar density, additional textural elements. Cumulative emotional weight builds through repetition.
multiple_chorus_iterations_with_intensif.applicability_text: Extended-form anthemic compositions; tracks where chorus repetition is the build mechanism; songs willing to scale chorus density across iterations.
multiple_chorus_iterations_with_intensif.trade_offs: Risk of feeling repetitive if intensification subtle. Demands strong chorus material that bears repetition.
multiple_chorus_iterations_with_intensif.documented_example: We're In This Together final chorus repetitions intensifying across 7:16 runtime.

production_vocabulary_compositional_stru.rule: Treat PRODUCTION VOCABULARY (industrial vs. rock vs. atmospheric vs. electronic toolkits) and COMPOSITIONAL STRUCTURE (anthem vs. dynamic-arc vs. atmospheric vs. minimalist) as INDEPENDENT AXES. Mix and match: industrial vocabulary in anthem structure, or atmospheric vocabulary in dynamic-arc structure, etc. Crossing the axes creates novel compositional fusion.
production_vocabulary_compositional_stru.applicability_text: Genre-flexible projects; productions willing to cross-pollinate vocabulary + structure independently; auteur-driven contexts.
production_vocabulary_compositional_stru.trade_offs: Demands cross-axis fluency; risks feeling forced if balance off. Demands artistic confidence.
production_vocabulary_compositional_stru.documented_example: We're In This Together — industrial vocabulary axis × anthem structure axis = novel fusion.

moulder_anthemic_industrial_mix_hybrid_b.rule: When mixing industrial-vocabulary anthem tracks, BALANCE rock-anthem stereo width with industrial-rhythm clarity. Wide enough for anthemic impact, tight enough for rhythm articulation. Multiple chorus vocal layering for anthemic fullness; minimum vocal pitch correction for emotional rawness.
moulder_anthemic_industrial_mix_hybrid_b.applicability_text: Hybrid industrial-anthem mix sessions; tracks bridging Wallace-style rock-wide and Moulder-style industrial-tight aesthetics.
moulder_anthemic_industrial_mix_hybrid_b.trade_offs: Demands mix engineer with both rock + industrial fluency. Risk of leaning too hard one direction.
moulder_anthemic_industrial_mix_hybrid_b.documented_example: Alan Moulder's We're In This Together mix — anthemic-industrial hybrid balance.

hybrid_vocabulary_integration_within_sec.rule: Within a single song, INTEGRATE two opposite genre-vocabularies (e.g., industrial aggressive + atmospheric sustain) so they coexist + transform within sections rather than alternating as separate sections. Sections may emphasize one vocabulary but contain elements from the other. Transitions feel INTEGRATED rather than ALTERNATING.
hybrid_vocabulary_integration_within_sec.applicability_text: Genre-fusion songs seeking integration not alternation; tracks where vocabularies coexist + transform within sections; productions willing to invest in deep cross-vocabulary blending.
hybrid_vocabulary_integration_within_sec.trade_offs: Risk of feeling unfocused if integration weak. Demands compositional + production sophistication.

section_specific_processing_chains_per_g.rule: Track + mix elements with DIFFERENT PROCESSING CHAINS for different sections of same song — vocal saturated in verses + clean in choruses, drums punchy in verses + atmospheric in choruses, etc. The processing IS the vocabulary signal to the listener.
section_specific_processing_chains_per_g.applicability_text: Hybrid-vocabulary recordings; tracks where processing differentiates sections; productions willing to commit to multi-chain workflows.
section_specific_processing_chains_per_g.trade_offs: Time-intensive tracking + mixing; requires production planning. Risk of processing inconsistency.
section_specific_processing_chains_per_g.documented_example: Wretched verse vs. chorus processing — Reznor's adaptive processing approach.

extensive_section_specific_mix_automatio.rule: When mixing hybrid-vocabulary songs, use EXTENSIVE section-specific mix automation — drum bus character, guitar layer levels, vocal processing, reverb sends all SHIFT between sections. Mix automation IS COMPOSITION at the production stage.
extensive_section_specific_mix_automatio.applicability_text: Mix sessions for hybrid-vocabulary tracks; mixers willing to invest in extensive automation; tracks where character shifts must read clearly.
extensive_section_specific_mix_automatio.trade_offs: Time-intensive mixing; requires mixer who understands compositional intent.
extensive_section_specific_mix_automatio.documented_example: Alan Moulder's Wretched mix — extensive section-specific automation for hybrid character management.

integrated_transitions_not_abrupt_cuts.rule: Build section transitions as INTEGRATED TRANSFORMATIONS rather than ABRUPT CUTS. Section A vocabulary BLEEDS INTO section B; section B retains residue from section A. The two vocabularies INTERPENETRATE rather than DISPLACE each other. Listener experience: gradual shift rather than jump-cut.
integrated_transitions_not_abrupt_cuts.applicability_text: Hybrid-vocabulary songs avoiding hard cuts; tracks where vocabularies should flow into each other; compositions valuing gradual transformation.
integrated_transitions_not_abrupt_cuts.trade_offs: Demands compositional confidence + production patience. Risk of muddiness if not executed precisely.
integrated_transitions_not_abrupt_cuts.documented_example: Wretched verse-chorus transitions — integrated rather than abrupt.

auteur_framework_third_compositional_int.rule: Demonstrate auteur range by exploring THREE OR MORE compositional intents within same album session — atmospheric vulnerability + anthemic emotional power + hybrid integration + aggressive extremity. Same auteur framework, multiple opposite-pole intents, single album.
auteur_framework_third_compositional_int.applicability_text: Multi-track albums by auteurs willing to demonstrate full range; sessions with diverse compositional intents; established artists challenging audience expectations within single album.
auteur_framework_third_compositional_int.trade_offs: Demands extended album form. Risk of audience confusion if range too wide. Demands artistic confidence to challenge audience expectations.
auteur_framework_third_compositional_int.documented_example: The Fragile (NIN 1999) — TDTWWA atmospheric + We're In This Together anthemic + Wretched hybrid + multiple aggressive tracks. Reznor framework supporting 4+ compositional intents.

vocal_hybrid_delivery_within_single_song.rule: Have vocalist execute HYBRID DELIVERY within same song — urgent moderate-aggression in verses + melodic sustained in choruses. Different vocal techniques + processing chains for different sections. Vocal hybrid carries song's emotional integration.
vocal_hybrid_delivery_within_single_song.applicability_text: Hybrid-vocabulary songs needing vocal range across sections; vocalists with multi-register capacity; productions willing to track multiple vocal chains.
vocal_hybrid_delivery_within_single_song.trade_offs: Requires vocalist with both capacities. Demands production fluidity.
vocal_hybrid_delivery_within_single_song.documented_example: Reznor's Wretched vocal hybrid — urgent verses + melodic choruses.

live_drums_with_programmed_overlay_hybri.rule: Build a track where LIVE DRUMS (rock drummer) are the rhythmic foundation, with programmed sample triggers + industrial percussion BLENDED on top as overlay. Live drums give ROCK-PHYSICAL energy; programmed elements retain industrial-production identity. Hybrid approach distinct from pure-programmed (Spiral) or pure-live (rock band) paradigms.
live_drums_with_programmed_overlay_hybri.applicability_text: Industrial-rock projects expanding to live drums; bands wanting rock-physical energy + industrial overlay; productions with available rock drummer.
live_drums_with_programmed_overlay_hybri.trade_offs: Requires available rock drummer; mix challenges integrating live + programmed without losing either character.
live_drums_with_programmed_overlay_hybri.documented_example: Hand That Feeds (NIN With Teeth 2005) — Dave Grohl live drums + Reznor programmed/sample overlay.

auteur_framework_expanded_collaboration_.rule: Expand auteur-producer framework via EXPANDED COLLABORATION (live drummer, production partner, mix engineer) to recalibrate toward commercial accessibility WITHOUT losing auteur identity. Auteur retains creative direction; collaborators bring specific skills + energies.
auteur_framework_expanded_collaboration_.applicability_text: Established auteur-producers seeking accessibility evolution; multi-album catalog at recalibration moment; auteurs willing to collaborate without diluting identity.
auteur_framework_expanded_collaboration_.trade_offs: Risk of identity dilution if expansion too aggressive. Requires auteur with strong creative center.
auteur_framework_expanded_collaboration_.documented_example: Reznor's With Teeth (2005) — auteur + Grohl + emerging Ross + Moulder. Expanded collaboration, retained Reznor identity.

commercial_single_economy_three_minutes_.rule: Compose to TIGHT COMMERCIAL-SINGLE ECONOMY (3-4 minutes) when the goal is radio + playlist accessibility. Verse-chorus-verse-chorus-bridge-chorus structure with hook emphasis. Skip extended sections. Compositional 'movement' compact + efficient. Trade extended-form ambition for accessibility breadth.
commercial_single_economy_three_minutes_.applicability_text: Commercial-single contexts; radio-ready material; tracks meant for playlist + algorithmic distribution.
commercial_single_economy_three_minutes_.trade_offs: Loses extended-form artistic ambition. May feel constrained for artists used to long-form expression.
commercial_single_economy_three_minutes_.documented_example: Hand That Feeds 3:31 — commercial-single economy after Fragile's extended-form ambition.

rock_drummer_in_industrial_production_co.rule: Hire a documented ROCK DRUMMER (Grohl, Chamberlin, etc.) to play within an industrial-production framework. The drummer brings ROCK-PHYSICAL energy that programmed-only production cannot replicate. Production framework must SUPPORT the live drummer rather than overwhelming them with overlay.
rock_drummer_in_industrial_production_co.applicability_text: Industrial productions wanting rock-physical drumming energy; bands willing to hire documented session drummer; tracks bridging programmed-only and rock-band paradigms.
rock_drummer_in_industrial_production_co.trade_offs: Drummer must adapt to industrial-context. Production must integrate live drumming without overwhelming.
rock_drummer_in_industrial_production_co.documented_example: Dave Grohl on NIN With Teeth (2005) — rock drummer in industrial context.

production_partnership_emerging_after_so.rule: After established solo-auteur period, INTRODUCE production partner who shares creative vision but brings specific complementary skills. Partnership should EVOLVE over multiple projects (emerging on album N, established on album N+1). Maintains auteur identity while distributing some production responsibilities.
production_partnership_emerging_after_so.applicability_text: Solo auteur-producers approaching collaboration evolution; multi-album projects where partner integration is gradual; auteurs willing to share creative space.
production_partnership_emerging_after_so.trade_offs: Risk of partnership dynamics issues. Demands auteur willingness to share creative space.
production_partnership_emerging_after_so.documented_example: Atticus Ross emerging as Reznor's production partner on With Teeth (2005), becoming full co-producer on subsequent albums.

loudness_war_mastering_balanced_with_dyn.rule: When mastering for 2000s+ commercial contexts (loudness-war era), aim for COMPETITIVE LOUDNESS while preserving as much DYNAMIC DETAIL as possible. Aggressive limiting + multiband compression standard for radio impact, but skilled mastering engineer (e.g., Tom Baker) preserves transient + dynamic detail within loudness budget.
loudness_war_mastering_balanced_with_dyn.applicability_text: 2000s-2010s commercial mastering; tracks targeting playlist + radio competitive loudness; mastering engineers with documented dynamic-preservation skill.
loudness_war_mastering_balanced_with_dyn.trade_offs: Loudness-war era inherently sacrifices dynamic range. Competitive loudness may damage mix character.
loudness_war_mastering_balanced_with_dyn.documented_example: Tom Baker's Hand That Feeds master — competitive 2005 loudness with preserved dynamic detail.

modular_electronic_patterns_as_compositi.rule: Use MODULAR ELECTRONIC PATTERNS (analog synths, modular synth elements, processed electronic textures) as primary compositional foundation. Patterns evolve via filter modulation + envelope changes rather than via traditional chord progression. Compositional 'movement' includes pattern-evolution + texture-transformation alongside vocal/melodic development.
modular_electronic_patterns_as_compositi.applicability_text: Modular-electronic compositions; auteur-producer projects with modular synth expertise; tracks targeting evolving-pattern compositional approach.
modular_electronic_patterns_as_compositi.trade_offs: Requires modular synth + electronic-music expertise. Less viable in pure-band-format contexts.
modular_electronic_patterns_as_compositi.documented_example: Copy of A (NIN, Hesitation Marks 2013) — modular-electronic patterns as compositional foundation.

film_scoring_discipline_integrated_into_.rule: When composing songs, apply FILM-SCORING DISCIPLINE — music as integrated whole, pattern evolution + texture transformation supporting emotional arc, less reliance on traditional song-structure templates. Trains compositional thinking beyond rock-band paradigm.
film_scoring_discipline_integrated_into_.applicability_text: Composers with film-scoring experience; songs benefiting from cinematic compositional thinking; productions willing to abandon song-template structure.
film_scoring_discipline_integrated_into_.trade_offs: Demands film-scoring experience. May feel less hooky/commercial than song-structure-template tracks.
film_scoring_discipline_integrated_into_.documented_example: Reznor + Ross's film-scoring work (Social Network, Girl with the Dragon Tattoo) discipline integrated into NIN's Hesitation Marks compositions.

auteur_co_auteur_mixer_pipeline_for_matu.rule: Establish AUTEUR + CO-AUTEUR + DEDICATED MIXER pipeline for mature collaborative production. Auteur retains creative direction, co-auteur shares production load + brings complementary perspective, dedicated mixer carries collaborative vision through to final master. Three-position pipeline distinct from band-democracy or producer-band-collaboration models.
auteur_co_auteur_mixer_pipeline_for_matu.applicability_text: Mature established auteur-producers; multi-album partnerships ready for full collaborative pipeline; productions willing to commit to three-position structure.
auteur_co_auteur_mixer_pipeline_for_matu.trade_offs: Requires established auteur + compatible co-auteur + dedicated mixer. Risk of pipeline rigidity if relationships strained.
auteur_co_auteur_mixer_pipeline_for_matu.documented_example: Reznor (auteur) + Ross (co-auteur) + Moulder (dedicated mixer) on Hesitation Marks (2013).

external_contributor_for_distinct_dimens.rule: Invite EXTERNAL CONTRIBUTOR with documented distinct musical identity (avant-rock guitarist, classical violinist, jazz horn player) to add DIMENSIONAL COLOR to specific tracks. Contributor's identity remains intact (their voice clearly heard) while serving the project's compositional intent.
external_contributor_for_distinct_dimens.applicability_text: Auteur projects benefiting from external dimensional color; tracks where guest contributor's identity is the dimension; productions willing to negotiate with established external artists.
external_contributor_for_distinct_dimens.trade_offs: Coordinating external contributors adds logistics. Risk of contributor's identity overwhelming project identity.
external_contributor_for_distinct_dimens.documented_example: Adrian Belew (King Crimson + Bowie + Talking Heads guitarist) contributing to Hesitation Marks (NIN 2013).

mature_vocal_aesthetic_post_aggression_p.rule: Once an artist has demonstrated AGGRESSION pole + VULNERABILITY pole vocal capacities, develop MATURE MELODIC pole — neither aggressive nor vulnerable but melodically deliberate, breath-supported, restrained-but-engaged. Demonstrates artistic range completion.
mature_vocal_aesthetic_post_aggression_p.applicability_text: Established vocalists with documented aggression + vulnerability poles; mid-to-late career projects; tracks where vocal restraint is artistic achievement not weakness.
mature_vocal_aesthetic_post_aggression_p.trade_offs: Requires extensive vocal range development across years. Demands artistic patience.
mature_vocal_aesthetic_post_aggression_p.documented_example: Reznor's Copy of A vocal — 2013 mature delivery distinct from 1994 aggression + 1999 vulnerability poles.

auteur_framework_evolution_across_decade.rule: Build AUTEUR FRAMEWORK EVOLUTION ARC across decades (15+ years). Each album demonstrates evolution while retaining auteur identity — pure auteur → auteur + mixer collaboration → auteur + emerging partner + live drums → auteur + co-auteur + mixer + external contributors. Framework EXPANDS without identity dilution.
auteur_framework_evolution_across_decade.applicability_text: Multi-decade auteur careers; long-term creative arcs; established artists planning catalog-level evolution.
auteur_framework_evolution_across_decade.trade_offs: Requires multi-decade artistic commitment. Risk of stagnation if evolution insufficient.
auteur_framework_evolution_across_decade.documented_example: Reznor's NIN catalog 1994-2013 — Spiral pure auteur, Fragile + Moulder mix, With Teeth + emerging Ross + Grohl drums, Hesitation Marks Reznor + Ross + Moulder + Belew. FOUR documented evolution points.

robot_rock_hypnotic_repetitive_riff_with.rule: Build composition on REPETITIVE riff that REPEATS NEAR-IDENTICALLY but never EXACTLY identically across measures. Micro-variations (slight timing shifts, occasional different pickup notes, subtle phrasing variations) create HYPNOTIC GROOVE that breathes. Riff should be playable + memorable but not require technical complexity.
robot_rock_hypnotic_repetitive_riff_with.applicability_text: Stoner-rock or desert-rock or groove-heavy alt-rock compositions; bands with riff-discipline + groove sensitivity; tracks where repetition IS the song.
robot_rock_hypnotic_repetitive_riff_with.trade_offs: Risk of feeling repetitive-without-engagement if micro-variations not carefully placed. Demands compositional discipline (don't add unnecessary sections).
robot_rock_hypnotic_repetitive_riff_with.documented_example: First It Giveth (QOTSA, Songs for the Deaf 2002) — Homme's documented robot-rock philosophy.

power_trio_plus_guest_harmony_arrangemen.rule: Build arrangement on TIGHT POWER TRIO foundation (guitar + bass + drums) + ADD ONE GUEST CONTRIBUTOR for dimensional color (vocal harmony, secondary instrument, etc.). Avoid wall-of-sound layering. Each element earns its presence; guest contributor adds specific dimension without dominating.
power_trio_plus_guest_harmony_arrangemen.applicability_text: Power-trio bands willing to feature one guest; productions valuing economy + dimensional color over wall-of-sound; tracks where trio identity is core.
power_trio_plus_guest_harmony_arrangemen.trade_offs: Demands strong individual musicians since fewer layers to hide behind. Risk of feeling thin if material weak.
power_trio_plus_guest_harmony_arrangemen.documented_example: First It Giveth: Homme + Oliveri + Grohl trio + Mark Lanegan harmony guest.

groove_over_complexity_compositional_pri.rule: Prioritize POCKET + FEEL over technical demonstration. Drummer plays FOR the groove, not for the spotlight. Bass + drums coordinate tightly. Guitar serves the riff + groove rather than showcasing chops. Vocal serves the song's groove energy.
